title: New Fiscal Year, Frozen Budgets
setup: It's early January, and half your pipeline of expected renewals has gone quiet. Buyers say their new fiscal-year budgets haven't been approved yet, and nobody can commit to signing until finance finalizes spend.
questions: Do you offer short-term extensions to bridge the gap, or hold firm on renewal dates?
How do you keep champions engaged during a budget freeze without seeming pushy?
Should your sales comp plan account for this seasonal Q1 slowdown?

title: A Free Competitor Shows Up in February
setup: A well-funded competitor announces a permanently free tier with features matching your $99/month plan. Your support team is already fielding 'why should I keep paying?' tickets.
questions: What in your product is genuinely defensible against a free alternative?
Do you respond with pricing changes, or double down on a different value proposition?
How do you brief your sales team before this becomes the top objection in every call?

title: The Quarter-Close Discount Spiral
setup: It's the last week of Q1. Three deals are stuck on price, and reps are asking for discounts deeper than anything approved this year to hit quota before the quarter closes.
questions: Where's the line between a healthy close-of-quarter push and training customers to wait for discounts?
Should approval authority for exceptions change for next quarter?
What does this reveal about how deals are priced earlier in the pipeline?

title: An Enterprise Deal Hinges on a Feature You Don't Have
setup: A large prospective account, mid-negotiation in April, says they'll only sign if you support SSO with their specific identity provider — a build you estimated at six weeks, and they want an answer in five days.
questions: Do you commit to the build before the contract is signed?
How do you decide if this becomes a roadmap priority or a one-off customization?
What do you tell other prospects who've asked for the same thing but aren't as far along?

title: A Competitor's Breach Puts You on the Spot
setup: In May, a direct competitor discloses a data breach affecting thousands of customers. Your own prospects and customers are now asking pointed questions about your security posture, and you don't have a SOC 2 report ready to share.
questions: How do you respond to security questions today without overpromising?
Does this change the priority of your compliance roadmap?
Is there a way to turn this into a trust-building moment rather than a defensive one?

title: Your Lead Engineer Leaves Mid-Release
setup: In June, the engineer who owns your biggest upcoming release gives two weeks' notice, right as the feature enters final testing before a customer-promised ship date.
questions: Do you slip the release date or ship with reduced confidence?
How exposed is the team to single points of failure like this one?
What do you tell the customers who were promised this feature by a specific date?

title: The Summer Usage Dip
setup: It's July, and product usage metrics are down 15% as customers' teams take vacation. Meanwhile, annual contracts signed a year ago in July are now up for renewal, and low recent usage is making renewal conversations harder.
questions: How do you separate a seasonal usage dip from a real engagement problem in renewal conversations?
Should renewal timing be smoothed across the year, or is this just a cost of doing business?
What proactive outreach could have prevented this renewal risk from being a surprise?

title: The Conference Announcement Falls Flat
setup: At your industry's biggest August conference, you unveil the feature you've spent six months building. The audience reaction is lukewarm, and a competitor's booth two aisles down is generating far more buzz with a smaller, simpler feature.
questions: Do you take the muted reaction as a signal to change course, or stay the course?
What does the competitor's simpler feature tell you about what the market actually wants?
How do you message this internally to a team that spent months on the launch?

title: Enterprise Prospects Start Their Q4 Evaluation
setup: September marks the start of enterprise budget planning season, and suddenly five large prospects want technical deep-dives and security reviews simultaneously, all wanting to close before their fiscal year ends in Q4.
questions: How do you prioritize which deals get the most solutions-engineering attention?
Is your team resourced to run five parallel enterprise evaluations at once?
What happens to your other pipeline while the team is focused on these deals?

title: A Critical Vulnerability in a Shared Dependency
setup: In October, a critical CVE is disclosed in an open-source library your product depends on. Customers are asking for a patch timeline within hours, and the fix requires touching a core piece of infrastructure.
questions: What's your process for triaging and communicating during an active vulnerability disclosure?
How much detail do you share publicly versus only with affected customers?
Does this expose a gap in how you track third-party dependencies?

title: The Q4 Renewal Crunch
setup: It's November, and forty annual contracts are up for renewal before year-end. Your customer success team, already stretched thin, is triaging which accounts get proactive outreach and which get a standard renewal email.
questions: How should renewal risk be scored to prioritize limited CS bandwidth?
What would it take to smooth renewal dates more evenly across the calendar next year?
Which of these forty accounts would you personally call first, and why?

title: A Multi-Year Deal Wants a Steep Discount
setup: In December, a large customer offers to sign a three-year contract if you cut annual pricing by 30%, wanting to lock it in before their own fiscal year-end. Finance is skeptical, but the ARR looks great on paper.
questions: How do you weigh locked-in revenue against margin erosion over three years?
What protections would you want in the contract if pricing or product scope changes?
Does saying yes here set a precedent other large accounts will expect?

title: A Critical Bug During the Holiday Code Freeze
setup: Your engineering team enacted a code freeze for the last two weeks of December, standard practice to avoid holiday deploy risk. Then a customer reports a bug that's blocking their year-end reporting, due in three days.
questions: Under what conditions is a code freeze worth breaking?
How do you weigh the risk of a rushed deploy against the risk of an unhappy customer at year-end?
Should critical-severity exceptions have been defined before the freeze started?

title: A New Privacy Regulation Lands in January
setup: A new data privacy regulation takes effect in a key market on January 1st, and your legal team just flagged that your current data handling doesn't fully comply. Enforcement begins immediately.
questions: How do you triage compliance work against the rest of the roadmap?
What's the cost of moving slowly versus the cost of rushing a compliance fix?
Should this change how you evaluate regulatory risk before entering new markets?

title: An Unsolicited Acquisition Offer
setup: Mid-year, a much larger company makes an informal acquisition offer, well above your last valuation, wanting an answer within two weeks.
questions: What would you need to know before taking this seriously?
How do you keep the team focused if word starts to leak?
What does 'walking away' cost you if this is the best offer you'll ever get?

title: January's Churn Wave Has a Pattern
setup: New-fiscal-year housecleaning brings January cancellations 2x the monthly average — and exit surveys show 60% cite 'consolidating tools.' Your product sits in a category CFOs increasingly see as a feature, not a platform.
questions: Is this seasonal churn or the leading edge of category consolidation?
What would make you the tool others get consolidated into?
Which at-risk accounts do you fight for, and which do you let go profitably?

title: The February Board Meeting Number
setup: Your first board meeting of the year is in ten days. Q4 bookings beat plan, but net revenue retention slipped below 100% for the first time — and one board member has been vocal that NRR is the only number that matters at your stage.
questions: Do you lead with the beat or the slip?
What's the honest diagnosis of the NRR decline — pricing, product, or customer mix?
What would you commit to fixing by the next meeting, and is it actually fixable in a quarter?

title: Usage-Based Pricing Backfires in Budget Season
setup: In March, your largest usage-based customer gets an invoice 3x their monthly norm after a successful product launch drove their traffic. Their procurement team calls it a 'billing incident' and demands a cap — mid-contract.
questions: Do you cap them and set a precedent, or hold terms and risk the renewal?
Is a bill spike from customer success actually a failure of your pricing model's design?
What would a pricing structure that scales with customer success — without punishing it — look like?

title: The April Fools Feature Announcement That Wasn't
setup: A joke feature your team announced on April 1st gets an overwhelming 'wait, we'd actually pay for that' response — thousands of signups to a waitlist for a product that doesn't exist.
questions: Is this real demand signal or costless enthusiasm that vanishes at a price point?
Do you build it, and what gets displaced from the roadmap if so?
How do you convert the waitlist's energy before it cools, even without a product?

title: A Partner Becomes a Competitor
setup: In May, the platform partner that drives 30% of your new signups announces a native feature that overlaps your core use case, launching at their June conference.
questions: Do you deepen the partnership, diversify channels hard, or start building off-platform?
What's defensible when the platform owns the customer relationship and the distribution?
How much runway do you have before the native feature is good enough for most users?

title: The Mid-Year Price Increase Memo
setup: Finance models show a 12% price increase in July would fund the hiring plan without new capital. Support tickets are stable, NPS is fine, but you haven't raised prices in three years and the customer base doesn't know what a price increase from you looks like.
questions: Grandfather existing customers, phase them in, or one clean increase for everyone?
What value story has to land before the email does?
What churn rate would make the increase a mistake, and what's your early-warning signal?

title: August's Quiet Feature Launch Data
setup: You shipped your biggest feature of the year into August's low-usage season. Adoption is 8% after three weeks. The team argues it's the vacation dip; the data team notes trial users in the same period adopted at 20%.
questions: What does the trial-versus-existing gap actually tell you?
Was August the wrong launch month, or is the feature discoverable only to people learning the product fresh?
Do you relaunch it in September or fix the in-product discovery first?

title: The September Analyst Report Snub
setup: The industry analyst report your enterprise buyers read before every Q4 evaluation just published — and dropped you from the leaders' quadrant, citing 'unclear enterprise roadmap.' Three active deals ask about it within a week.
questions: How do you handle the report in live deals — rebut, reframe, or redirect to references?
Is the analyst wrong, or is the enterprise roadmap actually unclear?
What's the year-long analyst-relations motion that prevents this next cycle?

title: The Q4 Pipeline Is Three Deals
setup: It's October and 70% of your Q4 number sits in three enterprise deals. Each has a champion, a budget, and a security review that could slip into January for reasons entirely outside your control.
questions: What de-risking can you actually do — parallel paths, exec sponsorship, contract pre-work?
Do you build a Q4 plan that survives losing one of the three?
How did the pipeline get this concentrated, and what changes about next year's coverage model?

title: The Holiday On-Call Rotation Revolt
setup: In November, three senior engineers decline the December holiday on-call rotation, citing burnout from a year of incidents. Your biggest customers are retailers whose peak season is exactly that window.
questions: What makes holiday on-call worth volunteering for — pay, comp days, or fixing why incidents happen?
Is the real problem the rotation or the incident rate underneath it?
What do you owe the retail customers whose peak you cover, and does your staffing reflect it?

title: The Year-End Data Deletion Request
setup: In December, a churned enterprise customer invokes their contract's data-deletion clause — and your engineers discover backups aren't architected for single-tenant deletion without a costly manual process nobody scoped.
questions: What do you tell the customer about timeline and method, and how honest is it?
Is this a one-off engineering project or a compliance debt affecting every future churn?
What contract language should sales stop agreeing to until the architecture catches up?

title: Your Champion Just Got Laid Off
setup: January layoffs at your biggest account eliminate your executive champion and half the team using your product. The new budget owner inherits your renewal — due in March — with no relationship and a mandate to cut spend 20%.
questions: How do you build a relationship with the new owner in eight weeks, starting from a cost line item?
What usage evidence survives the team shrinking by half?
Should your customer success model track champion risk the way sales tracks deal risk?

title: The Free Tier Is Growing the Wrong Way
setup: By spring, your free tier has doubled — but conversion to paid fell from 4% to 1.8%. Infrastructure costs for free users now exceed the marketing budget the tier was meant to replace.
questions: Is the free tier a funnel or a leak, and what evidence decides it?
What limits would push serious users to convert without gutting the tier's acquisition value?
If you tightened it, which of today's free users would you actually miss?

title: The June SOC 2 Audit Finding
setup: Your annual SOC 2 audit surfaces a finding: a contractor retained production access for four months after their engagement ended. Nothing was touched, but the finding goes in the report your Q4 enterprise prospects will all read.
questions: Do you flag it proactively in active deals or answer only when asked?
What's the remediation story that turns the finding into a maturity signal?
What does the offboarding failure say about what else the audits aren't catching?

title: The AI Rewrite Question
setup: It's mid-year and your board asks the question every SaaS board is asking: could an AI-native startup rebuild your core workflow product in a quarter? Your honest internal assessment is 'most of it, yes.'
questions: What in the product — data, integrations, trust, workflow depth — is actually hard to rebuild?
Do you disrupt your own interface before someone else does, and what does that break?
If you were founding a competitor today, what would you build first — and why isn't that your roadmap?

title: New Tariffs Hit Raw Material Costs
setup: New tariffs take effect January 1st on imported steel, raising your input costs 20% overnight. Fixed-price contracts signed last year don't allow for cost pass-through.
questions: Do you absorb the cost, renegotiate existing contracts, or find alternate suppliers?
How quickly could you qualify a domestic supplier as an alternative?
Should future contracts include a tariff/commodity price escalation clause?

title: Lunar New Year Shuts Down Your Supplier
setup: Your primary overseas component supplier closes for two weeks over Lunar New Year in February, as it does every year — but this year a shipping delay means the closure will push a customer's critical order three weeks late.
questions: Should this annual shutdown have been built into your planning calendar already?
How do you communicate the delay to the affected customer before they find out themselves?
Is it time to qualify a second-source supplier in a different time zone for exactly this reason?

title: A Surprise Safety Inspection
setup: An OSHA inspector arrives unannounced in March and identifies violations serious enough to threaten a partial shutdown of one production line, right in the middle of a big order.
questions: What's your immediate response to minimize both safety risk and production loss?
How do you communicate this internally without causing panic?
What does this reveal about your ongoing safety audit cadence?

title: A Commodity Price Spike Squeezes Margins
setup: In April, copper prices spike 25% in a month due to global supply disruptions. You have several fixed-price customer contracts running through the end of the year that didn't anticipate this.
questions: Do you honor the fixed prices at a loss, or attempt to renegotiate mid-contract?
How exposed are you to commodity swings across your whole contract book?
Should hedging or shorter contract terms be part of your strategy going forward?

title: A Strike Threat Right Before Peak Production
setup: Your union contract expires in May, right as you're ramping toward your busiest production months. Negotiations have stalled, and workers are discussing a strike authorization vote.
questions: How do you plan for continuity if a strike happens during peak season?
What's the cost of a short-term concession versus a prolonged work stoppage?
Should contract renewal timing be renegotiated to avoid this seasonal collision in the future?

title: The Summer Retooling Runs Long
setup: Your planned June shutdown to retool a production line for a new product was scheduled for one week. It's now day twelve, and customer orders are backing up.
questions: At what point do you communicate the delay to affected customers versus hoping to catch up?
What contingency capacity exists elsewhere in your network to absorb the backlog?
What went wrong in the retooling estimate, and how do you plan differently next time?

title: A Hurricane Threatens a Gulf Coast Supplier
setup: It's late July, and a hurricane is forecast to hit the region where your key chemical supplier operates within 72 hours. That supplier accounts for a third of your critical input material.
questions: Do you rush an emergency order now, and from where?
How much buffer inventory should you be carrying for suppliers in hurricane-prone regions?
What's your communication plan for customers if this supplier goes offline for weeks?

title: A Rival Undercuts You at the Trade Show
setup: At a major August trade show, a competitor unveils a similar product at a significantly lower price point, and two of your longtime distributors are already asking for a price match.
questions: Do you match the price, or defend on quality and reliability?
What does the competitor's pricing suggest about their cost structure or margins?
How do you keep distributor relationships steady while you figure out a response?

title: The Holiday Retail Rush Order
setup: It's September, and a major retail customer places a rush order 40% larger than forecast, needed in time for their holiday shelf-stocking in October — with only six weeks of lead time instead of the usual ten.
questions: Can you compress the timeline without sacrificing quality or overtime costs?
What does this do to your other scheduled production commitments?
Should large seasonal customers be required to commit to forecasts earlier?

title: A Recall on a Six-Month-Old Component
setup: In October, a safety issue is discovered in a component you shipped six months ago, now installed in thousands of units in the field. A recall is likely required.
questions: What's your immediate containment plan to stop further shipments?
How do you prioritize which customers or units get remediated first?
What does this cost in trust, and how do you rebuild it?

title: A Year-End Order Wants a Steep Discount
setup: In November, a major customer wants to finalize a large capital equipment order before their fiscal year closes on December 31st, but is asking for a 15% discount to sign now.
questions: Is the certainty of closing this quarter worth the margin given up?
What else could you offer instead of a straight discount — extended terms, faster delivery, service credits?
Does this customer's year-end timing pattern repeat, and should you plan for it next year?

title: Holiday Shutdown Collides With an Urgent Order
setup: Your plant's traditional two-week December holiday shutdown is set, matching most of your workforce's expectations. Then a key customer requests an urgent order that would require running through part of that window.
questions: Do you ask for volunteer overtime, or hold the line on the shutdown schedule?
How do you weigh the customer relationship against employee morale and planned time off?
Should shutdown dates be more flexible or communicated further in advance to customers?

title: Winter Energy Prices Squeeze the Budget
setup: A cold snap in December drives regional energy prices up sharply, and your energy-intensive production lines are running well over the budgeted operating cost for the month.
questions: Do you pass this cost through, absorb it, or slow production during peak-price hours?
Would locking in energy contracts earlier in the year have protected you here?
How much of your cost structure is exposed to this kind of seasonal volatility?

title: New ESG Reporting Requirements Arrive
setup: A new regulation taking effect this January requires detailed carbon emissions disclosure for your sector, and your current data collection isn't set up to produce it.
questions: How do you prioritize this against ongoing production and cost pressures?
What's the risk of non-compliance versus the cost of building out this reporting quickly?
Could better data infrastructure here also reveal cost-saving opportunities?

title: A Key Supplier Files for Bankruptcy
setup: In March, your sole-source supplier for a critical component announces they've filed for Chapter 11, with an uncertain future for continued operations.
questions: How quickly can you identify and qualify an alternative supplier?
Should you have avoided sole-sourcing this component in the first place?
What's your message to customers if this creates a supply gap in the coming months?

title: January's Annual Price Letter War
setup: Your annual price-increase letters went out January 2nd: 6% across the board. Your largest customer — 20% of revenue — responded with a counter-letter demanding a 3% decrease, citing two competitors' quotes.
questions: Is the quote threat real, and how do you find out before blinking?
What does the relationship history earn either side in this negotiation?
If you lose 20% of revenue, what's the actual timeline and cost to backfill it?

title: The February Capex Freeze
setup: Corporate freezes capital spending in February after a weak Q4 — including the already-approved replacement for a press that maintenance rates as 'run to failure within 12 months.' Peak production season starts in five months.
questions: Do you fight the freeze with a risk case, or build a failure contingency plan?
What does an unplanned failure in peak season cost versus the capex being frozen?
Can maintenance investment buy enough reliability to bridge a year?

title: The March Quality Escape
setup: A customer's incoming inspection catches a dimensional defect in a March shipment — the third escape in six months from the same cell. They're now inspecting 100% of your parts and billing you for it, and their annual supplier scorecard locks in April.
questions: What does the scorecard hit cost you in next year's volume allocation?
Is the cell's problem equipment, method, or a metric that rewards speed over quality?
Do you go to the customer with a corrective plan before they ask for one?

title: April's Apprenticeship Decision
setup: Half your skilled machinists retire within eight years, and this April's trade-school graduating class is your best recruiting window of the year. A real apprenticeship program costs $80k per year and three years per machinist — and your competitor poaches trained people at year two.
questions: How do you build training that survives poaching — contracts, culture, or pay progression?
What's the true cost of not training, paid in overtime and turned-down orders?
Could a shared regional program with other manufacturers beat going alone?

title: The May Freight Contract Renewal
setup: Your annual freight contract renews in May, and carriers are quoting 15% increases citing fuel and driver costs. Your sales quotes for the next year already assume flat freight.
questions: Do you lock a year at +15%, go spot-market and gamble, or split the difference?
Whose margin absorbs the gap on quotes already committed?
Should freight assumptions in quotes carry an escalator the way materials increasingly do?

title: The June Insourcing Pitch
setup: A team lead proposes insourcing a machined component you've bought for years, showing a 30% piece-cost saving. The catch: it needs a machine purchase, six months of ramp, and the current supplier also feeds you two other critical parts.
questions: What does the supplier relationship risk add to the insourcing business case?
Is the 30% saving real once ramp scrap, maintenance, and opportunity cost land?
What signal does insourcing send your other suppliers about their future?

title: July's Heat Wave Hits the Floor
setup: A July heat wave pushes shop-floor temperatures past 95°F. Productivity drops, two workers go home with heat symptoms, and the only quotes for cooling the high-bay space are eye-watering. August is usually hotter.
questions: What's the immediate playbook — shifted hours, rotation, cooling stations — for the next six weeks?
Where's the line between comfort spending and safety obligation?
Is climate-driven summer heat now an annual operating condition your budget should assume?

title: The August Cyber Insurance Questionnaire
setup: Your cyber insurance renewal questionnaire arrives in August with new requirements: network segmentation between IT and the production floor, and MFA on all systems. Your 20-year-old machine controllers can't do either. Non-compliance triples the premium — or voids coverage.
questions: Do you pay the premium, invest in segmentation, or self-insure the OT risk?
What would a ransomware stop of the production floor actually cost per week?
Whose budget does OT security belong to — IT, operations, or risk?

title: September's Double Broker Discovery
setup: A late shipment investigation in September reveals your freight broker has been double-brokering your loads to unvetted carriers. One of those carriers just had an uninsured accident with your goods aboard.
questions: What's your recovery path for the lost load, and who actually owes you?
Do you fire the broker mid-peak-season with holiday shipping ahead?
What carrier-vetting requirements belong in your freight contracts from now on?

title: The October Inventory Write-Down
setup: Pre-audit inventory review in October finds $2M of raw material bought during the shortage panic two years ago, now worth half its book value and physically in the way of holiday production flow.
questions: Do you take the write-down this fiscal year or carry the fiction another year?
What buying-policy changes stop the next panic-buy — and would you actually follow them in the next shortage?
Can any of the material be reworked, resold, or traded rather than written off?

title: November's Overtime Fatigue Signal
setup: Six weeks into the year-end production push, November's numbers show overtime at 28% — and recordable incidents at double the annual rate, all in overtime hours. Two more heavy weeks are scheduled.
questions: Is the correlation between fatigue and incidents enough to change the schedule this week?
What's the incident trend worth against the delivery commitments the overtime protects?
What capacity decision made last spring created this November?

title: The December Vendor Gift Audit
setup: Your new compliance officer flags December's tradition: purchasing staff receiving substantial holiday gifts from suppliers — tickets, cases of wine, one weekend trip. Nothing violates a written policy, because there isn't one.
questions: Where's the line between relationship customs and influence, and who draws it?
Do you write the policy effective now, mid-December, or January 1st?
Does purchasing's supplier-selection history need a look, and how do you do that without accusing anyone?

title: The New Year Reshoring Inquiry
setup: In January, a Fortune 500 company you've never worked with asks if you could take on reshored volume that would double your revenue — with a decision needed by March and production by September. Their last supplier relationship famously ended with a brutal cost-down squeeze.
questions: Can you scale that fast without betting the company on one customer?
What terms protect you from becoming the next squeezed supplier?
Do your existing customers suffer during the ramp, and which ones can least afford to?

title: February's Predictive Maintenance Pilot Verdict
setup: Your year-long predictive maintenance pilot on Line 2 just closed with February's report: downtime fell 18%, but the sensor platform's annual license across all lines would cost more than last year's total unplanned-downtime losses.
questions: Was Line 2's improvement the sensors, or the attention the pilot brought?
Is there a cheaper path to the same result — better PM discipline, selective sensors on critical assets?
What would the vendor's price need to be, and do you negotiate or walk?

title: The Spring Flood Plain Letter
setup: In March, your property insurer reclassifies your 40-year-old plant site into a higher flood-risk category, raising premiums 60% and hinting at future non-renewal. Relocation is a $30M, three-year proposition; the river hasn't flooded since 1987.
questions: Do you trust the insurer's climate model or the 40-year track record?
What flood mitigation investment would change the classification, and is it cheaper than the premium delta?
At what point does a paid-off plant in the wrong place become the expensive option?

title: New Reimbursement Rates Cut Revenue
setup: New CMS reimbursement rates take effect January 1st, cutting payment for several of your highest-volume procedures by double digits, with no advance grace period.
questions: Which service lines are now operating at a loss, and what's the plan for them?
Do you adjust patient volume, staffing, or pricing on non-covered services to compensate?
How do you communicate this financial pressure to physicians without affecting care quality?

title: Flu Season Overwhelms Capacity
setup: A severe flu season in February is filling beds faster than expected, forcing you to consider postponing elective procedures to free up capacity.
questions: How do you decide which elective procedures get postponed and communicate that to patients?
What's the financial impact of delayed elective revenue versus emergency capacity needs?
Should seasonal staffing plans have anticipated this surge better?

title: The EHR Migration Falls Behind
setup: Your new electronic health records system was supposed to go live in March. Two weeks before cutover, testing reveals data migration errors that could delay the launch by months.
questions: Do you delay the go-live, or proceed and fix issues in production?
What's the clinical risk of a rushed transition versus the cost of further delay?
How do you keep staff confident in a system they've already been trained on once?

title: A Malpractice Claim Before Accreditation Review
setup: In April, a malpractice claim is filed against a senior physician just three weeks before your facility's accreditation survey is scheduled.
questions: How do you manage the claim process without it affecting the accreditation review?
What does this reveal about physician oversight processes that need strengthening now?
How transparent should you be with surveyors about an active claim?

title: A Key Specialist Resigns in Hiring Season
setup: In May, your only specialist in a high-demand field resigns with a month's notice, right as recruiting for the year's most competitive hiring season is underway across the industry.
questions: How do you cover patient needs during the gap without a replacement?
What would make your organization competitive in this tight hiring market?
Should key specialties have succession or backup coverage plans already?

title: The Summer Elective Surgery Lull
setup: Elective surgery volume drops sharply in June and July as patients delay procedures for summer vacations, leaving your surgical staff overstaffed relative to current demand.
questions: Do you adjust staffing levels seasonally, or absorb the cost to keep the team ready?
Could scheduling incentives shift some of this demand into the slower months?
How predictable is this seasonal pattern, and is it already built into your annual budget?

title: A Price Transparency Deadline Arrives
setup: A new price transparency regulation requires you to publicly post rates for common procedures starting in July, and your billing team hasn't finished calculating accurate figures.
questions: What's the risk of posting estimates versus missing the compliance deadline entirely?
How might publishing these rates affect competition with nearby providers?
Does this expose pricing inconsistencies you'll need to address internally first?

title: The Back-to-School Vaccination Rush
setup: August brings a predictable surge in vaccination and physical exam appointments ahead of the school year, and your scheduling system is already showing a two-week backlog.
questions: Could extended hours or a temporary clinic absorb this seasonal surge?
How do you prioritize urgent versus routine appointments during the crunch?
Is this predictable enough that next year's staffing should be planned differently?

title: A Data Breach Right Before Flu Season
setup: In September, a cybersecurity breach exposes patient records just as your team is gearing up operationally for flu season preparations.
questions: How do you run breach notification and remediation without derailing seasonal readiness?
What's your communication plan for patients whose data was exposed?
Does this reveal a security gap that needs fixing before more sensitive systems are touched?

title: An Insurer Drops You From Network During Open Enrollment
setup: In October, right as open enrollment begins, a major insurer notifies you they're dropping your facility from their network next year. Patients are already calling, confused and anxious.
questions: What's your immediate messaging to patients who now face an in-network decision?
Is there room to renegotiate before enrollment periods close?
How dependent is your revenue on this single payer relationship?

title: Holiday Staffing Shortages Meet Flu Season
setup: In November, requested holiday time off leaves you short-staffed just as flu season admissions begin climbing, and current staff are already reporting burnout.
questions: How do you balance honoring time-off requests with patient safety needs?
What temporary or float staffing options could bridge this gap?
Should holiday staffing policy change to better anticipate this seasonal collision?

title: A Year-End Budget Cut Mid-Flu-Season
setup: In December, the hospital board demands immediate cost cuts to meet year-end financial targets, right in the middle of the flu season admission peak.
questions: Which costs can be cut without compromising patient care during peak demand?
How do you push back on timing if the cuts genuinely risk care quality?
Should budget target deadlines account for known seasonal demand patterns?

title: A New Urgent Care Opens Across the Street
setup: In January, a well-funded urgent care chain opens a location directly across from your facility, offering shorter wait times and extended hours.
questions: What does your patient volume data suggest about who might switch, and why?
Can you compete on convenience, or is your differentiation elsewhere?
Should this change how you invest in scheduling and wait-time improvements?

title: A Telehealth Policy Change Threatens Revenue
setup: A policy change in February ends expanded telehealth reimbursement that had become a meaningful revenue stream since it was introduced, with 60 days' notice.
questions: How much of your current revenue and patient access depends on this reimbursement?
Can telehealth services shift to a different payment model in time?
What does this teach you about building revenue on policy that can change quickly?

title: A Community Screening Event Cuts Both Ways
setup: A free community health screening event in May draws far more attendees than expected, generating great local press — but also surfaces several urgent findings your follow-up capacity wasn't sized for.
questions: How do you handle the immediate care needs surfaced by the event responsibly?
Does the PR value justify the strain on clinical resources it created?
Should future events be sized differently, or is this a good problem to have?

title: January's Deductible Reset Collections
setup: Deductibles reset January 1st, and by month's end patient-responsibility balances are triple the fall average. Front-desk staff are undertrained for financial conversations, and complaints about 'surprise' costs are climbing.
questions: How do you collect effectively without making the front desk feel like a collections agency?
What pre-visit cost transparency would prevent the surprise in the first place?
Should January staffing include dedicated financial counselors, given this happens every year?

title: The February Locum Bill
setup: Covering winter vacancies with locum physicians is costing 2.5x employed-physician rates, and February's numbers show locums now covering 20% of shifts. Recruiting says permanent hires are 9-12 months out.
questions: Is the locum spend a bridge or a business model you've backed into?
What makes permanent roles here attractive enough to close in months, not a year?
Which service lines could safely reduce coverage instead of buying it at 2.5x?

title: March Madness in the ED
setup: Your emergency department's March data shows 30% of visits were treatable in primary care — but your own primary care practices have three-week waits, and the community's two walk-in clinics closed last year.
questions: Is ED overuse a patient-behavior problem or an access-design problem you own?
What would same-week primary care access cost versus what avoidable ED visits cost?
Does an urgent-care tier belong in your system, even if it cannibalizes ED revenue?

title: The April Prior Authorization Pile-Up
setup: A major insurer's new prior-authorization requirements, effective April 1st, add review steps to procedures that were previously automatic. Your surgical schedule is developing holes as authorizations lag, and staff overtime for appeals paperwork is climbing.
questions: Do you staff up for the paperwork war or push back through the payer relationship?
What do schedule holes cost versus the administrative staffing to prevent them?
Which procedures should shift to payers with saner authorization rules, where patients allow?

title: May's Nurse Residency Cohort Decision
setup: Nursing school graduation is in May, and your residency program can take 20 new grads. Last year's cohort had 30% leave within 18 months — mostly to a competitor paying $8k more after you paid for their first-year training.
questions: Do retention contracts for trained nurses help or poison the well?
What did exit interviews say the $8k was actually standing in for?
Is training the region's nurses a cost to minimize or an identity to leverage?

title: The June Ransomware Tabletop Goes Badly
setup: Your June disaster exercise simulates ransomware taking down the EHR for 72 hours. The result: departments couldn't locate paper forms, two units had no downtime procedures at all, and lab results routing collapsed in the first simulated hour.
questions: What's the minimum viable paper workflow every unit must prove quarterly?
Does this result change your cyber insurance, your backup architecture, or both?
How do you keep downtime readiness alive when the EHR works fine 99.9% of the time?

title: July's New Residents and the Safety Data
setup: It's July — new residents start, and your quality team quietly tracks the well-documented seasonal uptick in medication errors and near-misses that follows. This year a board member read about the 'July effect' and wants a formal response.
questions: What supervision structures actually blunt the July effect versus just adding paperwork?
How transparent should you be with patients about the seasonal reality of teaching hospitals?
What does the near-miss data from past Julys say you should change this one?

title: The August Charity Care Headline
setup: A local newspaper's August investigation names your hospital among nonprofits whose charity care spending trails their tax exemption's value, with a patient's billing story as the centerpiece. A city council member is now questioning your property tax status.
questions: Is the criticism substantively fair, and does your answer change the response strategy?
What charity-care and billing changes would you defend making regardless of the pressure?
Who tells your community-benefit story publicly, and why wasn't it landing before this?

title: September's Specialist Poaching Offer
setup: A private equity-backed specialty group offers to acquire your three-physician cardiology practice in September — the physicians are tempted, and losing them means losing the cath lab volume that subsidizes two unprofitable service lines.
questions: What can you offer that competes with a PE buyout — and should you?
If they go, what's the real timeline before cath lab volume follows them?
Which subsidized service lines survive without cardiology's contribution margin?

title: The October Flu Shot Standoff
setup: Your October employee flu vaccination rate is 71% against a system target of 90%, and this year's early flu indicators are bad. The options on the table range from incentives to mask mandates for the unvaccinated to making it a condition of employment.
questions: Which lever gets you to 90% with the least damage to trust and retention?
What does staff vaccination actually protect — patients, operations, or optics — and does the answer change the policy?
How did peer hospitals that hit 90% get there?

title: November's Observation Status Complaints
setup: Patient complaints about 'observation status' — hospitalized but technically outpatient, with the different billing that follows — spike in November as Medicare open enrollment gets seniors reading their statements closely. Your observation rates are within norms, but nobody explains status to patients at admission.
questions: Whose job is the observation-status conversation, and when should it happen?
Are your observation-status decisions clinically driven or billing-driven, and can you prove it?
What would it cost to just communicate this well, versus what the complaints cost?

title: The December 31st Surgery Rush
setup: The last two weeks of December bring the annual surge of patients who've met their deductibles racing to schedule procedures before the reset. Your ORs are booked past capacity, staff want holiday time, and January's schedule is nearly empty.
questions: How much December demand can you safely absorb before quality risk outweighs revenue?
Could pricing or scheduling incentives pull some deductible-driven demand into January?
Is the December-crush-January-crater cycle something to manage or something to redesign around?

title: The January Weight-Loss Drug Formulary Call
setup: New-year demand for GLP-1 weight-loss prescriptions is overwhelming your primary care practices, and your employed-physician group needs a consistent stance: your own employee health plan is also projecting an eight-figure cost if uptake continues.
questions: Can your clinical stance and your benefits-cost stance on the same drugs honestly differ?
What's the criteria framework that keeps prescribing consistent across 40 physicians?
How do you handle the patients who'll go to telehealth prescribers if you say no?

title: February's AI Scribe Pilot Results
setup: Your three-month AI documentation scribe pilot ends in February: physicians using it report 45 fewer minutes of after-hours charting daily and love it — but coding review found it occasionally omits billable complexity, costing an estimated 4% of revenue per encounter.
questions: Is 45 minutes of physician time worth 4% of encounter revenue — and whose call is that?
Can the coding gap be fixed with review workflow, or is it inherent to the tool?
If you roll it out, do you credit the time savings toward more patients or less burnout?

title: The Spring Ratings Drop
setup: CMS star ratings refresh in spring, and your flagship hospital drops from four stars to three, driven by readmission data from a period two years ago — before improvements you've already made. Competitors' marketing teams notice within a week.
questions: How do you counter a public rating you believe is stale without sounding defensive?
Which referring physicians and payers actually change behavior on a star rating, and what do they need to hear?
What's the internal reaction — urgency or injustice — and which one does the culture reward?

title: Tax Season Refund Surge
setup: It's late January, and tax refund season is approaching. Historical data shows a major spike in mobile deposit volume in February, and your systems team hasn't finished this year's capacity testing.
questions: What's the risk of a system slowdown during the highest-traffic weeks of the year?
Is capacity testing for this predictable seasonal spike happening early enough each year?
How do you communicate proactively if any slowdown does occur?

title: The Fed Moves Rates Without Warning
setup: The Federal Reserve announces a surprise rate change in February. Your deposit and loan products need repricing, and competitors are already advertising new rates within hours.
questions: How fast can you reprice products without eroding margins or losing depositors?
What's your communication plan for existing customers with rate-sensitive products?
Does your rate-change response process need to be faster given how quickly competitors moved?

title: The Spring Mortgage Rush
setup: Spring home-buying season kicks off in March, and mortgage applications have jumped 40% week-over-week, well beyond what your underwriting team can process at normal turnaround times.
questions: Do you bring in temporary underwriting support, or let turnaround times slip?
How do you prioritize which applications move fastest without appearing unfair?
Is seasonal staffing for this predictable rush already built into your annual plan?

title: Fraud Spikes Around Tax Refunds
setup: In April, fraud attempts targeting tax refund deposits spike sharply, a pattern that recurs every year around this time, and your fraud team is flagging a rise in false positives too.
questions: How do you tune fraud detection for this seasonal spike without frustrating legitimate customers?
What's the cost of a false positive versus a missed fraud attempt during this window?
Should this seasonal pattern trigger a temporary staffing or rules change every year?

title: A Fintech Undercuts Your Savings Rate
setup: In May, a well-marketed fintech competitor launches a high-yield savings account offering nearly double your best rate, and you're seeing early signs of deposit outflow.
questions: Do you match the rate, or compete on trust, branch access, and service instead?
How much deposit outflow would justify a rate response versus riding it out?
What does this reveal about which customer segments are most rate-sensitive?

title: A Regulatory Exam With Short Notice
setup: In June, regulators announce an exam with only 30 days' notice, and an internal review turns up compliance gaps in a lending process that's been in place for over a year.
questions: How do you prioritize remediation in the time available before the exam?
Should these gaps be proactively disclosed, or fixed quietly before the exam begins?
What does this reveal about the cadence of your internal compliance reviews?

title: Summer Travel Fraud Spike
setup: July brings a predictable rise in cross-border card transactions as customers travel, along with a corresponding spike in fraud flags and legitimate transactions getting blocked abroad.
questions: How do you balance fraud protection against the frustration of blocked legitimate travel purchases?
Could proactive travel notifications reduce false declines during this season?
Is your fraud model retrained often enough to reflect this yearly travel pattern?

title: Back-to-School Loan Demand
setup: In August, personal and student loan applications surge as families prepare for the school year, and underwriting turnaround times are slipping past your service standards.
questions: Is this predictable seasonal demand reflected in your annual staffing plan?
What's the cost of a slow turnaround during this competitive lending season?
Could a streamlined process for smaller, common loan types free up capacity?

title: A Peer Bank's Breach Raises Questions
setup: In September, a competitor bank discloses a data breach. Your call centers are suddenly fielding a wave of customers asking whether their own accounts are safe.
questions: How do you reassure customers without overstating your own security posture?
Is this a moment to proactively communicate your security practices, or stay quiet?
What would you actually do differently if the breach had happened to you?

title: Aggressive Student Loan Refinancing Competition
setup: In October, a competitor launches an aggressive student loan refinancing campaign timed to fall enrollment decisions, undercutting your rates by a full point.
questions: Do you match the rate, or emphasize service and flexibility instead?
How much of your loan book is exposed to refinancing risk from a move like this?
Should your refinancing offers be more proactive rather than reactive to competitors?

title: Holiday Fraud Detection Overcorrects
setup: In November, holiday shopping season fraud rules trigger a surge of false positives, and customers are complaining loudly on social media about blocked legitimate purchases during peak shopping days.
questions: How quickly can fraud rules be recalibrated without reopening real fraud exposure?
What's the reputational cost of blocked purchases during the busiest shopping days of the year?
Should these rules be seasonally adjusted every year in advance rather than reactively?

title: Year-End Wealth Management Rush
setup: In December, high-net-worth clients flood your wealth management team with requests for tax-loss harvesting and year-end portfolio moves, all wanting execution before December 31st.
questions: Is your advisory team resourced for this predictable annual crunch?
How do you prioritize requests fairly when everyone wants same-day execution?
Could earlier client outreach in November have smoothed this demand?

title: A Branch Closure Decision Before Year-End
setup: Budget planning requires a decision on closing an underperforming branch before the fiscal year locks in December, but the branch serves a community with vocal local support and limited alternative access.
questions: How do you weigh community impact against the financial case for closure?
What alternatives (reduced hours, hybrid service) might satisfy both sides?
How do you communicate this decision if you proceed, to minimize backlash?

title: A New CD Promotion Draws Away Deposits
setup: Right at the start of the year, a competitor launches an aggressive CD rate promotion, and you're seeing early signs of deposit attrition among rate-sensitive customers.
questions: Do you launch a competing promotion, or accept some deposit attrition as the cost of stable pricing?
How much of your deposit base is genuinely at risk versus just rate-shopping?
What non-rate factors could you lean on to retain these customers?

title: A Major Commercial Borrower Fails
setup: In March, a significant commercial lending client's business collapses unexpectedly, putting a large loan at risk of default and raising questions about concentration risk in your loan portfolio.
questions: What's the immediate exposure, and how do you work the recovery process?
Does this reveal a concentration risk that needs addressing across other large loans?
How do you communicate this risk internally and to regulators if required?

title: January's Branch Traffic Cliff
setup: Post-holiday January branch traffic is down 30% from a year ago, continuing a steady migration to digital — but the customers still coming in skew older and hold your highest deposit balances.
questions: How do you cut branch cost without alienating the customers who fund your balance sheet?
What does the branch mean to a high-balance customer who rarely uses it — and does closing it break something invisible?
Is there a branch format between full-service and closed?

title: The February Overdraft Fee Decision
setup: Two major national banks eliminated overdraft fees in January, and your February board meeting will discuss following. Overdraft revenue is 4% of your income; the customers who pay it skew toward your most financially fragile.
questions: Is overdraft revenue worth the reputational and regulatory trajectory it sits on?
What replaces the revenue — and does the replacement just move the burden elsewhere?
If you keep fees, what's the defensible version — caps, grace periods, real-time alerts?

title: March's Commercial Real Estate Review
setup: Your March portfolio review flags that office-building loans — 15% of your commercial book — face a wall of maturities over 18 months, in a market where those buildings appraise 30% below origination values.
questions: Do you extend and pretend, restructure proactively, or take losses early on the worst credits?
What does the regulator expect to see in your CRE workout playbook before they ask?
Which borrowers are worth partnering through the trough, and which are already gone?

title: The April Teller Turnover Problem
setup: Spring hiring season data shows teller turnover hit 40% annually — every retailer and warehouse in town pays comparable wages for less emotional labor. Each departure costs an estimated $15k in hiring and training.
questions: Is the teller role as currently designed simply obsolete at this wage?
What would a career-path version of the front line look like, and what does it cost versus 40% turnover?
Which branch tasks could automation absorb so fewer, better-paid people handle the rest?

title: May's Zelle Scam Wave
setup: May brings a wave of customers scammed into authorizing instant transfers — romance scams, fake fraud-department calls. Legally you may owe them nothing, since they authorized the payments. Three cases are now with a local TV consumer reporter.
questions: Where do you land between legal obligation and customer protection — and can you afford your answer at scale?
What friction in the payment flow would stop scams without killing the product's point?
How do you get ahead of the TV story rather than respond to it?

title: The June Core Banking Decision
setup: Your core banking system contract renews in June for five years. The incumbent is stable, expensive, and slow to ship features; the modern challenger is cheaper and faster but has never converted a bank your size. Every fintech partnership you want is easier on the new core.
questions: What does five more years on the old core cost in products you can't launch?
What's the honest failure probability of a core conversion, and what does failure look like?
Is there a middle path — sidecar core for new products, legacy for the base?

title: July's Deposit Concentration Warning
setup: A quiet July risk review finds that your ten largest depositors now hold 18% of total deposits, most of it uninsured, most of it rate-shopping money that arrived during last year's CD promotion.
questions: How fast could that money leave, and what's the liquidity plan for the day it does?
Do you pay up to retain hot money or let it run off and shrink deliberately?
What did the CD promotion actually buy you, knowing what you know now?

title: The August Small Business Credit Box
setup: August lending data shows your small-business approval rate fell to 31% as underwriting tightened — while a fintech lender approves your declined applicants in hours at higher rates, and several of those businesses moved their deposits there too.
questions: Is the tight credit box protecting you or handing your customer base to competitors loan by loan?
Could you serve the declined tier yourself with risk-adjusted pricing rather than a no?
What's a small-business deposit relationship worth in your lifetime-value math when the loan gets declined?

title: September's Branch Manager Exodus
setup: Three of your twelve branch managers resign in September — all to the same competitor, all citing decision authority: your model routes every exception to regional office, theirs empowers the branch.
questions: Is centralized control saving you more in risk than it's costing you in people?
What decisions could move to branch level tomorrow without real risk?
What do the remaining nine managers need to hear this week, and from whom?

title: The October Check Fraud Surge
setup: Mailbox check theft and washing has made October's fraud losses triple last year's, concentrated in business accounts still paying vendors by mail. Positive-pay tools exist, but only 12% of your business customers have adopted them.
questions: Do you make positive pay free, default, or mandatory for accounts writing checks?
Whose loss is a washed check in practice, and does your answer survive a big client dispute?
Is this the moment to push business customers off checks entirely?

title: November's Card Rewards Math
setup: Ahead of holiday spending season, November's program review shows your flagship rewards card loses money on 30% of cardholders — the optimizers who redeem everything and revolve nothing — while the profitable revolvers subsidize them.
questions: Do you re-tier the rewards knowing the optimizers are also your most vocal advocates?
Is a card that profits only from customers carrying debt the business you want?
What would the program look like designed around your actual margin, not the competitor's marketing?

title: The December CRA Exam Prep
setup: Your Community Reinvestment Act exam is scheduled for Q1, and December's internal review shows lending in two low-income census tracts has quietly fallen near zero since a branch closure there three years ago.
questions: What can credibly change in one quarter, and what needs a multi-year commitment the examiners will test later?
Was the branch closure's community impact assessed honestly at the time — and who asks that question about the next closure?
What partnerships could serve those tracts better than the branch did?

title: The New Year Data-Sharing Mandate
setup: Open-banking rules taking effect this year require you to share customer data with authorized third parties via API. Compliance work is budgeted — but January strategy discussions keep treating it as pure cost, while two competitors are building products on other banks' data.
questions: Are you building the minimum pipe or a position in the new plumbing?
What products could you build on data flowing toward you rather than away?
Which of your customers are most likely to aggregate away, and what keeps them?

title: February's ATM Fleet Verdict
setup: Your ATM fleet needs a $4M refresh cycle decision by February. Cash withdrawals are down 40% over five years, but the machines are also your only physical presence in six communities where branches already closed.
questions: Is an ATM a cash dispenser or a brand outpost, and does $4M make sense under either answer?
What does exiting cash access do to the communities — and to your CRA standing?
Could shared-fleet or white-label arrangements keep the presence at half the cost?

title: The Spring Deepfake Authorization
setup: In March, a business customer's controller authorizes a $240k wire after a video call with someone who looked and sounded exactly like her CEO. It was a deepfake. The customer says your verification procedures should have caught it; your procedures were followed to the letter.
questions: Whose loss is this, legally and relationally — and are those answers different?
What verification protocol survives deepfakes, and how fast can you deploy it?
Do you warn all business customers about this now, knowing it invites 'why weren't you protecting us already?'

title: The Post-Holiday Slow Season
setup: It's mid-January, occupancy has dropped sharply after the holidays, and finance wants cost cuts — but summer's peak season hiring will need to ramp back up in just a few months.
questions: How do you cut costs now without losing staff you'll need to rehire and retrain later?
What off-season revenue opportunities (events, local packages) could offset the dip?
Is seasonal staffing structured to flex efficiently, or is this pain avoidable with better planning?

title: A Winter Storm Cancels a Major Booking
setup: A winter storm in February forces a large conference booking to cancel with two days' notice, and your contract's weather cancellation terms are ambiguous about refunds.
questions: How do you handle the refund conversation while protecting the relationship for future bookings?
Does this expose a gap in your contract language that needs fixing going forward?
What can you do with the suddenly open dates on short notice?

title: The Spring Break Surge
setup: Spring break bookings in March have filled the property to capacity, but front desk and housekeeping staffing levels were set based on last year's numbers, which were 20% lower.
questions: Can you bring in temporary staff on short notice without sacrificing service quality?
How do you keep existing staff from burning out during the surge?
Should next year's seasonal staffing plan use more conservative or aggressive booking assumptions?

title: A Minimum Wage Increase Hits Before Peak Hiring
setup: A state minimum wage increase takes effect in April, right as you're preparing to hire seasonal staff for the summer peak, raising labor costs beyond what was budgeted.
questions: Do you adjust room rates to offset the increased labor cost, or absorb it?
How does this change the economics of the seasonal staffing plan you'd already built?
Could efficiency changes elsewhere offset some of the added labor cost?

title: A Double-Booking on the Biggest Wedding Weekend
setup: Peak wedding season begins in May, and a scheduling error surfaces: two clients believe they've booked your venue's only ballroom for the same Saturday, both non-refundable deposits paid months ago.
questions: How do you resolve this fairly without losing both clients' trust?
What compensation or alternatives could make this right for whichever party can't keep the date?
What booking system safeguard should have caught this earlier?

title: The GM Resigns at Peak Season
setup: Your general manager resigns with two weeks' notice in June, right as the property enters its highest-occupancy months of the year.
questions: Who steps in during the search, and are they ready for peak-season pressure?
How do you keep the team steady through a leadership transition at the worst possible time?
Should key-role succession planning have been in place before this happened?

title: A Viral Review Hits During Peak Season
setup: A widely shared negative review — exaggerated but not entirely false — goes viral in July, cutting new bookings by over a third during your busiest month of the year.
questions: What's your first public response, and who approves it?
How do you rebuild trust with guests who already have upcoming reservations?
What operational gap did the review expose that's worth fixing regardless of the PR angle?

title: A Hurricane Threatens Peak Summer Bookings
setup: A hurricane is forecast to make landfall near your coastal property in August, right during your highest-revenue week of the year, forcing an evacuation decision with guests already checked in.
questions: At what point do you initiate evacuation versus wait for more certainty?
How do you handle refunds and rebooking for a mandatory evacuation?
Is your severe weather protocol tested and ready, or being figured out in real time?

title: AV Equipment Fails Mid-Conference
setup: A major corporate conference booking in September, the largest of the fall season, suffers a complete AV system failure ten minutes before the keynote presentation.
questions: What's your emergency backup plan when core equipment fails during a live event?
How do you make this right with the client after the event, beyond an apology?
Does this reveal a need for redundant systems for high-value bookings?

title: The Off-Season Renovation Dilemma
setup: As the slower October season approaches, you must decide whether to close part of the property for a needed renovation now, or wait until after the upcoming holiday season and risk running it during next year's peak.
questions: What's the revenue cost of renovating now versus the risk of delaying into peak season?
How do you communicate a temporary closure to guests with existing reservations?
Could the renovation be phased to avoid a full closure altogether?

title: Thanksgiving Travel Strains a Short-Staffed Team
setup: Thanksgiving week in November brings a major travel surge right after a slow October led you to trim seasonal staff, leaving the property short-handed for one of the year's busiest travel weeks.
questions: Can you quickly bring back recently trimmed staff, or is it too late?
How do you prioritize guest service with a leaner team than the volume demands?
Should staffing cuts have anticipated this immediate seasonal rebound?

title: A Vendor Cancels Days Before New Year's Eve
setup: Your New Year's Eve event has sold out, but the entertainment vendor cancels four days before the event, citing a scheduling conflict.
questions: Can you find a suitable replacement on such short notice, and at what cost?
How do you communicate any changes to ticket holders without causing a wave of refund requests?
Should future high-stakes event contracts include stronger cancellation penalties?

title: Staff Request Time Off During the Busiest Week
setup: The week between Christmas and New Year's is historically your highest-occupancy week, and this year an unusually high number of staff have requested that exact week off.
questions: How do you balance staff wellbeing with the operational needs of your busiest week?
Should holiday time-off policy be restructured to prevent this collision every year?
What incentives could encourage voluntary coverage without resentment?

title: A Loyalty Program Overhaul Confuses Guests
setup: You relaunch your loyalty program in January with new tiers and rules, timed for the new year — but it goes live the same weekend as a large holiday-carryover booking rush, and confused returning guests are flooding the front desk with complaints.
questions: Should the launch have been timed away from a known high-traffic weekend?
How do you handle guests who feel they lost benefits they were expecting?
What's the fastest way to clarify the new program without a full re-launch?

title: The Summer Corporate Travel Dip
setup: Your downtown business hotel relies heavily on weekday corporate travel, which drops sharply every July as business trips slow for summer, leaving rooms empty despite leisure travel booming elsewhere.
questions: Could you capture some of the leisure travel demand that's currently going to other properties?
Is this seasonal dip predictable enough to plan promotions around in advance?
What would it take to diversify away from dependence on corporate travel alone?

title: January's Dry Month Squeeze
setup: Dry January has grown every year, and this year your hotel bar and restaurant revenue is down 25% for the month. Your beverage program is your highest-margin operation, and February looks only slightly better.
questions: Do you fight the trend or build a serious non-alcoholic program with real margins?
What January programming could replace the bar revenue — wellness packages, dining events?
Is this a January problem or an early signal about your core beverage margins long-term?

title: The Valentine's Double-Seating Gamble
setup: Valentine's Day falls on a Saturday this year. Your restaurant can either sell one leisurely seating at a premium prix fixe or two turns at higher total revenue but rushed service — on the one night guests most remember how it felt.
questions: Which does your brand sell — the evening or the table?
What does a rushed anniversary dinner cost you in reviews and returns versus the second seating's revenue?
Could a bar-area tasting experience capture overflow demand without compromising the dining room?

title: March's OTA Dependence Reckoning
setup: Your Q1 channel report lands in March: online travel agencies now deliver 65% of bookings at 18% commission, up from 50% two years ago. Direct booking incentives haven't moved the number, and your best OTA just raised its commission tier.
questions: What would actually shift share to direct — price, perks, or product only bookable with you?
Is fighting OTA share even right, or should you optimize within their ecosystem?
What's the lifetime-value difference between an OTA guest and a direct guest, really?

title: The April Group Block Walk-Back
setup: A corporate group holding 60% of your rooms for a late-April conference cuts its block in half three weeks out — inside the contract's reduction window, so no penalty applies. Those dates were turned away to other business months ago.
questions: Can you refill half the house in three weeks, and at what rate desperation?
Should future group contracts trade lower rates for firmer commitments?
How do you keep the client relationship warm while eating the consequences of their contract right?

title: May's Pool Lifeguard Mandate
setup: Three weeks before Memorial Day weekend opens pool season, your insurer mandates certified lifeguards during all pool hours — a cost you've never carried — after an industry-wide claims review. Certified guards are scarce in May because every property is hiring them.
questions: Do you staff guards, restrict pool hours, or challenge the mandate with your broker?
What does a diminished pool experience cost during the season guests book you for?
Is this a one-insurer quirk or where the whole market is heading — and does that change the response?

title: The June Wedding Party Damage Bill
setup: A June wedding party causes $30k in damage to your presidential suite and two floors. The father of the bride — a repeat corporate client worth six figures annually — says his card on file should not be charged and 'we'll work something out.'
questions: How do you price the damage against the relationship without setting a precedent that big clients break things free?
What does your event contract actually entitle you to, and does enforcing it end the corporate account?
What security deposit and monitoring changes do high-risk events need going forward?

title: July's Housekeeping Opt-Out Economics
setup: Your opt-out housekeeping program — pitched as green, adopted during labor shortages — now has July data: 40% of guests skip daily service, saving real labor cost, but deep-clean time between stays is up and guest cleanliness scores slipped two points.
questions: Is opt-out housekeeping a sustainability program or a service cut wearing one's clothes?
Do the labor savings survive the longer turnovers and the score decline?
What would you learn from making the incentive symmetrical — pay guests to skip, charge nothing to keep?

title: The August Extended-Stay Pivot Pitch
setup: An August strategy review shows your weakest property sits in a market flooded with new short-stay supply but starving for extended-stay inventory — travel nurses, relocations, project crews. Conversion means kitchenettes, different staffing, and exiting the nightly-rate business you know.
questions: What evidence would make you confident the extended-stay demand is durable, not cyclical?
Which of your current team and systems transfer, and which have to be rebuilt?
Is a struggling property the right conversion candidate or just the available one?

title: September's Conference Season No-Show Data
setup: Fall conference season opens and September's banquet data confirms a post-pandemic pattern: guaranteed-count no-show rates at group dinners have doubled, but clients still negotiate guarantees down and attrition clauses out, citing 'industry standard.'
questions: Do you hold the line on guarantees and lose bids, or price the no-show risk into every proposal?
What does the doubled no-show rate do to food cost, labor scheduling, and waste?
Could data-sharing with repeat clients turn guarantee-setting collaborative instead of adversarial?

title: The October Haunted Reputation
setup: A ghost-hunting web series films at your historic property in October without permission, and the episode goes viral. Paranormal tourism inquiries flood in — some guests are thrilled, but your bread-and-butter corporate clients are asking careful questions about 'brand fit.'
questions: Do you lean into the haunted angle seasonally, ignore it, or actively counter it?
Can one property serve ghost tourists in October and risk-averse corporate planners in November?
What's the legal and practical response to the unauthorized filming — and does fighting it just amplify the story?

title: November's Dynamic Pricing Backlash
setup: Your new revenue-management system tripled room rates for the weekend of a local disaster-relief effort in November — algorithmically correct, humanly terrible. Screenshots are circulating with the word 'gouging' before your team even noticed the rates.
questions: What pricing guardrails does the algorithm need for events where demand spikes for the wrong reasons?
How do you respond publicly today — apology, refunds, donated rooms?
Who owns algorithmic decisions between the software, revenue management, and the GM?

title: The December Party Season Staffing Auction
setup: Holiday party season books every event space in town, and December banquet staff have become free agents — three of your best servers just got offered $10/hour more for the exact dates you need them, by the hotel across the street.
questions: Do you match December premiums and normalize seasonal bidding wars, or hold pay scales and risk short crews?
What non-wage loyalty do you actually offer a banquet server — and would you stay for it?
Should peak-season pricing to clients rise to fund peak-season pay?

title: The New Year Resort Fee Reckoning
setup: New federal junk-fee rules effective January 1st require your resort fee in the advertised price. Displayed rates jump $45 overnight while competitors who never charged the fee suddenly look cheaper — even though total prices haven't changed at all.
questions: Do you fold the fee into the rate, kill it and raise rates transparently, or keep the newly-visible line item?
What did the resort fee actually fund, and does anyone believe that story?
How do you handle the booking-comparison disadvantage during the adjustment months?

title: February's Loyalty Points Devaluation Decision
setup: Your loyalty program's outstanding points liability has grown 35% and finance wants a February devaluation — raising redemption thresholds before spring booking season. The last devaluation, done quietly, generated a week of furious coverage from points bloggers.
questions: Is there a way to reduce liability that members experience as change, not theft?
What do your most valuable members actually redeem for, and can you protect just that?
Loudly with notice, or quietly and absorb the blogger storm — which costs less trust?

title: The Spring Break Age Policy
setup: After two years of spring break property damage and noise complaints driving off other guests, your Florida property proposes a 25+ age minimum for March bookings. Legal says it's permissible; revenue says it's 30% of March occupancy; the brand team is split.
questions: What does hosting spring break actually net after damage, comps, staff churn, and displaced guests?
Is an age minimum the tool, or is it pricing, deposits, and enforcement you've been unwilling to do?
What fills 30% of March if the spring breakers go — and how long does that transition take?

title: A Reimbursement Code Change Cuts Payment
setup: A new CMS reimbursement code takes effect January 1st, reducing payment for your flagship device by a meaningful margin, with hospital customers immediately pushing back on pricing.
questions: Do you adjust your price to hospitals, or help them navigate the new reimbursement reality?
How does this affect your sales team's pitch for the rest of the year?
Should future product pricing build in more buffer against reimbursement policy risk?

title: A Supplier Shutdown Collides With New Tariffs
setup: Your key overseas component supplier's annual Lunar New Year shutdown in February coincides this year with new tariffs on the same components, doubling the disruption to your production schedule.
questions: How much buffer inventory exists to absorb both disruptions at once?
Should sourcing strategy change given how these two annual/regulatory risks compounded?
What do you tell hospital customers awaiting scheduled deliveries?

title: An FDA Data Request Delays Launch
setup: Your FDA submission for a new device hits an unexpected data request in March, just weeks before a planned launch announcement at a major trade show.
questions: Do you delay the trade show announcement, or announce with a revised timeline?
How do you keep the sales pipeline warm during an uncertain approval delay?
What does this reveal about how launch timelines are set relative to regulatory risk?

title: A Cybersecurity Flaw in an Implanted Device
setup: In April, a security researcher discloses a vulnerability in the wireless connectivity of a device already implanted in thousands of patients.
questions: What's the immediate patient safety communication plan?
How do you coordinate disclosure with regulators and physicians simultaneously?
Does this change how connectivity features are designed and reviewed going forward?

title: A Distributor Relationship Sours Before Budget Season
setup: In May, your regional distributor relationship deteriorates over a pricing dispute, right as hospital systems in that region open their annual capital equipment budget cycle.
questions: Do you repair the relationship, or move to a direct sales model in that region quickly?
What's the risk of losing this budget cycle's opportunities during the transition?
Should distributor contracts include better provisions for disputes like this?

title: Mixed Clinical Trial Results
setup: In June, clinical trial results for your next-generation device come back mixed — meeting the primary endpoint but missing a secondary one investors and customers were watching closely.
questions: How do you communicate mixed results honestly without derailing the launch?
Does this change the go-to-market strategy or target patient population?
