What's the right level of transparency with physicians who were awaiting this data?

title: Patent Expiration Brings a Generic Overnight
setup: Your core device's patent expires in July, and a generic competitor enters the market within weeks at a third of your price.
questions: What justifies your price premium against a generic alternative?
Should you have prepared a next-generation product to launch ahead of this expiration?
How do you retain hospital contracts that are up for renewal in this same window?

title: A Rival Outshines You at the Trade Show
setup: At the industry's biggest August trade show, a competitor unveils a next-generation device with capabilities beyond your current roadmap, generating major buzz among the hospital buyers you were courting.
questions: Does this change your product roadmap priorities immediately?
How do you respond to customers who ask directly how you compare?
Is this a signal to accelerate R&D investment or to differentiate on a different axis entirely?

title: Flu Season Demand Outpaces Production
setup: As flu season ramps up in September, hospital demand for your respiratory support devices surges well beyond your current production capacity.
questions: How do you prioritize allocation across hospital customers fairly during a shortage?
Is this seasonal demand pattern predictable enough to plan production capacity around each year?
What's the cost of under-supplying versus over-investing in capacity for a seasonal peak?

title: Post-Market Surveillance Flags a Defect
setup: In October, post-market surveillance data reveals a defect rate above threshold in a component used across your device line, likely requiring a recall.
questions: What's your containment plan while the scope of the defect is being assessed?
How do you prioritize which units or customers get addressed first?
What does this mean for trust with hospital customers and regulators going forward?

title: Hospitals Rush Year-End Capital Purchases
setup: In November, hospital systems rushing to spend capital budgets before their fiscal year-end flood you with purchase orders your current lead times can't fulfill in time.
questions: How do you prioritize which orders can realistically be fulfilled by year-end?
What do you tell hospitals who won't get their equipment in time for their budget cycle?
Should production planning anticipate this predictable year-end demand spike earlier?

title: A Top Sales Rep Leaves Before Year-End Quota
setup: In December, your highest-performing sales rep resigns two weeks before year-end quota close, taking deep hospital relationships with them to a competitor.
questions: How do you protect at-risk hospital accounts during this transition?
Does this expose over-reliance on individual relationships rather than institutional ones?
What retention signals were missed that could have prevented this departure?

title: New Tariffs Land After Pricing Is Locked
setup: New tariffs on imported device components are announced in January, right after you've locked in annual pricing agreements with hospital systems for the year.
questions: Can existing contracts absorb this cost, or is there room to renegotiate mid-year?
Should future contracts include cost-escalation clauses for exactly this scenario?
How exposed is your supply chain to further tariff risk this year?

title: A Spring Screening Season Spike
setup: A pediatric-focused device sees a sharp demand spike each March tied to school health screening season, straining a supply chain sized for average monthly volume.
questions: Is this seasonal spike predictable enough to plan inventory buffers around each year?
How do you prioritize shipments when demand exceeds available stock?
Could better forecasting turn this into a planned opportunity rather than a scramble?

title: A Patent Lawsuit Mid-Fiscal-Year
setup: In June, a competitor files a patent infringement lawsuit against your core product line, threatening an injunction that could halt sales mid-fiscal-year.
questions: How do you communicate this risk to customers currently evaluating your product?
What's the contingency plan if an injunction is actually granted?
Does this change how aggressively you should have cleared IP risk before launch?

title: January's Sales Kickoff Comp Plan Revolt
setup: At January's national sales kickoff, the new comp plan lands badly: territory changes cut three top reps' account bases, and the hallway conversation is recruiters' InMails. Your Q1 pipeline lives in those reps' relationships.
questions: Do you adjust the plan publicly in week one, or hold firm and manage the fallout rep by rep?
What was the territory redesign meant to fix, and is there another way to fix it?
Which accounts survive a rep departure, and which walk out the door with them?

title: The February EU MDR Deadline Math
setup: February's regulatory review confirms it: recertifying your full catalog under EU MDR will cost more than several low-volume products earn in Europe. Discontinuing them abandons hospitals that depend on niche devices with no alternative supplier.
questions: Which products justify recertification on strategy rather than spreadsheet math?
What do you owe the hospitals using the products you sunset, and how much notice is enough?
Is there a partner or licensee model that keeps niche products alive without your regulatory burden?

title: March's Sterilization Plant Warning
setup: In March, the EPA signals tighter emissions rules for ethylene oxide sterilization facilities — and one of the two plants that sterilize your entire surgical line is a likely closure candidate. Industry-wide, capacity is already tight.
questions: How fast can you validate alternate sterilization methods or sites, given validation itself takes months?
Do you book backup capacity now at premium prices, before everyone else does?
Which products could redesign toward sterilization methods with a future?

title: The April AI Diagnostic Clearance Race
setup: In April, your main competitor receives FDA clearance for an AI-assisted diagnostic feature comparable to one sitting in your R&D backlog, deprioritized twice. Their press release names three of your flagship accounts as pilot sites.
questions: Is fast-following now the right move, or does their clearance change what you should build?
Why did your version get deprioritized twice, and were those reasons wrong?
What do you say to the three named accounts this week?

title: May's GPO Contract Cliff
setup: Your largest group purchasing organization contract — 30% of revenue — comes up for renewal in May, and the GPO wants an 8% price reduction plus a competitor added as dual source. Walking away means losing member hospitals' automatic access.
questions: What does dual-sourcing actually cost you in share versus what the 8% costs in margin?
Which member hospitals would stay with you off-contract, and at what price to serve them directly?
What differentiation would make the GPO need you more than you need them?

title: The June Physician Preference Battle
setup: A major hospital system's June value-analysis committee is standardizing your device category to a single vendor. Their surgeons prefer your device; their supply chain prefers your competitor's 15% lower price. The committee vote is in three weeks.
questions: Do you compete on price, or arm the surgeons with outcome data to fight the standardization?
What evidence actually moves a value-analysis committee versus what just moves surgeons?
If you lose this system, what's the contagion risk as other systems copy their analysis?

title: July's Field Inventory Audit Shock
setup: July's mid-year audit of field inventory — trunk stock and consignment at hospitals — finds 12% unaccounted for: expired, lost, or quietly used without purchase orders. That's seven figures of product and a compliance exposure if any reached patients past expiry.
questions: What's the immediate patient-safety obligation versus the financial cleanup?
Is consignment inventory a sales necessity or a control failure you've institutionalized?
What tracking would fix this, and why has the sales team resisted it?

title: The August Conference Data Leak
setup: Two weeks before the year's biggest clinical conference, an investigator site posts a slide with your embargoed pivotal trial results — positive ones — to a private physician forum, and it's already circulating. Your investors, competitors, and the journal reviewing your paper have all likely seen it.
questions: Does the leak force disclosure obligations if you're public or fundraising?
How do you protect the journal publication and the conference moment now?
What does the site's breach mean for the relationship, mid-trial, when you still need their patients?

title: September's Reprocessing Threat Assessment
setup: A third-party reprocessor gains clearance in September to reprocess your single-use device at 40% of your price, and your biggest hospital customers' sustainability offices are championing it internally.
questions: Do you fight reprocessing on safety evidence, compete with it on price, or enter reprocessing yourself?
Was single-use ever clinically necessary for this device, and can your answer survive scrutiny?
How does the sustainability argument change who you're really negotiating with inside the hospital?

title: The October Physician Payments Sunshine
setup: October's Open Payments database refresh lands, and a health-news outlet builds a story around your consulting payments to the surgeons who also published your device's favorable studies — all disclosed, all legal, all now framed as bought science.
questions: How do you defend legitimate physician collaboration without sounding like every company that said the same?
Do the named surgeons defend themselves, or does your silence protect or abandon them?
Should the consulting-and-research relationship structure change, not because it's illegal, but because it's unexplainable?

title: November's Home-Use Pivot Question
setup: Care keeps shifting home, and November's strategy review makes it concrete: your hospital-grade monitor could be redesigned for home use, but that means consumer usability standards, a new regulatory pathway, retail-adjacent distribution, and a price point one-tenth of hospital pricing.
questions: Is home health an extension of your business or a different business wearing your technology?
What dies in the hospital product line while you fund the home version?
Who wins home health if you don't enter — a rival, or a consumer electronics company?

title: The December Warranty Reserve Surprise
setup: Year-end close in December surfaces a problem: warranty claims on your two-year-old flagship are running 60% above the reserve model, driven by a component that degrades faster in high-utilization accounts — your best customers.
questions: Is this a warranty accounting fix, a proactive field action, or a looming recall — and who decides which?
Do you notify high-utilization accounts before failures, knowing it invites contract renegotiations?
What did the reliability testing miss about real-world duty cycles, and is it still missing it on the current product?

title: The January Cyber Disclosure Rule
setup: New FDA cybersecurity requirements effective this cycle demand a software bill of materials and coordinated disclosure process for your connected devices. January's engineering review reveals nobody can fully enumerate the third-party code inside your oldest connected product, which 400 hospitals still run.
questions: Can the legacy product be made compliant, or does it need a supported migration path off the market?
What do you tell the 400 hospitals about a device whose software contents you can't fully list?
How does software governance change so the next product doesn't become this one?

title: February's Clinical Trial Enrollment Stall
setup: Your post-market study — a condition of approval — is 40% behind enrollment at February's checkpoint. Sites blame competing trials and narrow criteria; the FDA milestone is in September, and missing it invites consequences ranging from label changes to withdrawal.
questions: Do you add sites, loosen criteria within protocol amendments, or pay sites more per enrollment — and what does each do to data quality?
When do you talk to the FDA — now with a recovery plan, or later with results?
What did the enrollment model assume that reality didn't deliver?

title: The Spring Tender Season Trap
setup: National tender season opens in several international markets each spring, and this year's largest tender requires local manufacturing within three years as a condition of award. It's 15% of your international revenue — and the local-manufacturing requirement is spreading to other countries' tenders.
questions: Is localized manufacturing an investment in market access or a ransom that every market will soon demand?
What's the minimum viable local operation that satisfies the requirement — assembly, packaging, full production?
Which international markets do you concede rather than localize for, and what's the strategic cost of shrinking your map?

title: A Rival Leapfrogs You at CES
setup: At January's biggest consumer tech show, a competitor unveils a product with features that leapfrog what you shipped just weeks earlier, dominating the show's headlines.
questions: Do you rush a response, or trust your existing roadmap timeline?
How do you keep customers and retail partners confident in your product while the news cycle favors a competitor?
What does this reveal about your competitive intelligence process before launches?

title: A Chip Shortage Delays Your Flagship Launch
setup: A global semiconductor shortage in February threatens to delay production of your flagship device just ahead of a heavily marketed launch window.
questions: Do you delay the launch, or ship a reduced initial quantity and manage expectations?
How exposed are you to a single chip supplier, and should that change?
What do you tell retail partners who have already committed shelf space and marketing?

title: A Software Update Breaks Your Hardware
setup: In March, a third-party operating system update breaks compatibility with your hardware, flooding support lines with complaints you didn't cause but must now fix.
questions: How fast can you ship a compatibility patch, and what's the interim guidance for customers?
Should you have a stronger relationship with the OS vendor to get advance notice of changes?
What does this reveal about testing against pre-release OS builds going forward?

title: Investors Punish a Slower Product Cycle
setup: During April earnings season, investors react sharply negative to news that your next major product cycle will take longer than the market expected, and the stock drops double digits.
questions: How do you communicate a longer roadmap without eroding further confidence?
Is the slower cycle actually the right call for product quality, regardless of market reaction?
What expectations were set too aggressively in the first place?

title: A Patent Lawsuit Threatens an Injunction
setup: In May, a competitor files a patent lawsuit seeking an injunction against your best-selling product, right as it's ramping toward its highest sales volume of the year.
questions: What's the contingency plan if an injunction is actually granted?
How do you communicate this legal risk to retail partners and customers?
Does this change how aggressively you clear IP risk on future products before launch?

title: A Manufacturing Partner Shuts Down for the Season
setup: Your key overseas manufacturing partner closes for a week-long national holiday in June, a predictable annual event that this year collides with a tight summer shipment deadline.
questions: Was this annual closure factored into your production planning far enough in advance?
What flexibility exists to shift production timing around known annual shutdowns?
How do you communicate any resulting delay to customers and retail partners?

title: Back-to-School Demand Meets a Shortage
setup: Back-to-school laptop demand surges in July, but ongoing component shortages mean you can't fully meet retailer orders during the season's critical buying window.
questions: How do you allocate limited supply fairly across retail partners?
What's the cost of missed back-to-school sales versus rushing unqualified components into production?
Should component inventory buffers be built up earlier for this predictable seasonal demand?

title: A Talent War Breaks Out Before a Fall Launch
setup: In August, a rival company launches an aggressive hiring campaign targeting your engineering team with large offers, right as your team is in the critical final push toward a fall product launch.
questions: How do you retain key engineers through the most critical weeks without overpaying reactively?
What does this reveal about compensation competitiveness that needs addressing structurally?
How exposed is the launch timeline if even one or two key engineers leave now?

title: A Manufacturing Defect Threatens the Launch Event
setup: Days before your major September product launch event, a manufacturing defect is discovered in a small percentage of units already produced for day-one availability.
questions: Do you delay day-one availability, or launch with a subset of confirmed-good units?
How transparent should the launch messaging be about this defect?
What does this mean for the retail partners counting on day-one inventory?

title: Holiday Season Demand Strains the Supply Chain
setup: In October, early holiday season ordering from retailers is running well above forecast, and your supply chain is being pushed to its limits to keep up before the critical November-December window.
questions: Do you prioritize retail partners by size, by long-term relationship, or by order date?
What's the risk of stockouts during the year's most important sales weeks?
Should forecasting models have caught this demand trend earlier in the year?

title: A Black Friday Website Crash
setup: Your e-commerce site crashes for two hours during peak Black Friday traffic in November, right as a heavily promoted deal was set to go live.
questions: What's the immediate customer communication and recovery plan?
How much revenue and trust was lost, and how do you make it right for affected customers?
Was load testing for this predictable annual traffic spike sufficient?

title: The Post-Holiday Return Surge
setup: In December, a wave of post-holiday returns and support requests overwhelms your customer service team, right as they're also managing year-end close processes.
questions: Is seasonal support staffing sized for this predictable annual surge?
What's driving the return volume, and is any of it addressable through product or packaging changes?
How do you keep response times reasonable without burning out the support team?

title: A New Sustainability Regulation Requires a Redesign
setup: A new e-waste and packaging regulation takes effect in January, requiring design changes to your product line before the next manufacturing cycle can begin.
questions: How do you prioritize this compliance work against other roadmap commitments?
Could sustainable redesign become a marketing advantage rather than just a compliance cost?
What's the cost of missing the regulatory deadline versus rushing the redesign?

title: A Tariff Hits After Pricing Is Set
setup: A new tariff on imported electronics components is announced in February, right after your annual pricing for the year has already been finalized and communicated to retailers.
questions: Do you absorb the cost for the rest of the pricing cycle, or attempt a mid-cycle price change?
How exposed is your supply chain to further tariff changes this year?
Should future pricing cycles build in more buffer for this kind of policy risk?

title: Pressure to Rush an AI Feature
setup: In August, competitors are generating major buzz around new AI features ahead of a major fall conference, and there's internal pressure to rush your own unfinished AI feature to keep pace, even though it's not fully tested.
questions: What's the risk of shipping an unfinished feature versus appearing behind the AI trend?
How do you decide what 'good enough for launch' actually means here?
Is chasing this hype cycle the right call, or a distraction from your core roadmap?

title: January's Post-CES Roadmap Panic
setup: Your team returns from CES in January with a competitive analysis that reads like an obituary: three rivals showed products your roadmap doesn't answer until next year. Engineering says the dates are the dates; sales is already fielding customer questions.
questions: What's real competitive threat versus trade-show vaporware, and how do you tell?
Is there a software or partnership answer that bridges the hardware gap?
How do you keep sales confident selling this year's product against next year's demos?

title: The February Component End-of-Life Notice
setup: A February end-of-life notice gives you nine months of last-time-buy availability on a component in your best-selling product. The redesign to a newer part needs twelve months. The gap is a seven-figure last-time-buy gamble on demand forecasts.
questions: How much last-time-buy inventory do you commit to, knowing both over- and under-buying are expensive?
Can the redesign compress to close the gap, and what does the compression risk?
Should component lifecycle monitoring have caught this at the design stage?

title: March's Channel Inventory Illusion
setup: Q1 sell-in numbers look strong heading into March quarter-close — but channel data shows distributors holding 14 weeks of inventory against a 8-week norm. Sales wants to ship the quarter; ops sees a Q2 cliff being built.
questions: Do you stuff the channel to make the quarter, knowing Q2 pays for it?
What incentives created 14 weeks of channel inventory, and who benefits from not noticing?
What does honest sell-through reporting change about how the street and the board read you?

title: The April Open Source License Bomb
setup: An April due-diligence scan — triggered by acquisition interest in your company — finds a copyleft-licensed library compiled into your flagship firmware. Full compliance could mean releasing proprietary source; the acquirer's counsel has flagged it as a deal issue.
questions: What's the remediation path — replace the library, negotiate a commercial license, or release the code?
Does this kill the acquisition timeline, and do you tell them your plan or wait for their ask?
What does engineering's license-review process look like after this?

title: May's Right-to-Repair Law
setup: A major state's right-to-repair law takes effect in May, requiring you to sell parts, tools, and documentation to independent shops. Your service revenue is 20% of margin, and your authorized-repair network — built over a decade — is asking what their exclusivity was worth.
questions: Do you comply minimally, or embrace repairability as brand strategy the way some rivals have?
What happens to service margin, and what replaces it?
What do you owe the authorized network that invested in your exclusivity model?

title: The June Datacenter Power Wall
setup: Your June infrastructure review lands hard: the AI features you've promised customers will triple your compute needs within 18 months, and your datacenter provider says grid constraints mean no new capacity in your region until two years out.
questions: Do you buy scarce capacity now at premium prices, re-architect for efficiency, or promise less AI?
Which promised features actually need the compute, and which are riding the AI label?
Does power availability now belong in product planning the way component supply does?

title: July's Grey Market Discovery
setup: July pricing analysis explains a regional sales slump: your own products, sold at distributor pricing into a low-price market, are being re-exported and undercutting your premium markets by 20%. One of your largest distributors appears to be the source.
questions: Do you confront the distributor with evidence, tighten contracts, or restructure regional pricing that created the arbitrage?
What does the 20% gap say about whether your regional pricing is defensible at all?
How do you police grey markets without alienating the channel you depend on?

title: The August Prototype Leak
setup: Photos of your unannounced flagship — scheduled for a September launch — leak from the supply chain in August, accurate down to the port layout. Preorders of the current model stall within days as customers wait for the new one.
questions: Do you accelerate the announcement, discount the current model, or hold the plan and absorb the Osborne effect?
Where in the supply chain did this leak, and can you actually find out?
Should launch timing assume leaks as a planning fact rather than treat them as surprises?

title: September's Subscription Pivot Resistance
setup: Your September board meeting approves pivoting flagship software from perpetual licenses to subscription — recurring revenue the street rewards. Early customer advisory feedback is blunt: several largest accounts call it 'paying again for what we own,' and one is scoping alternatives.
questions: What does the subscription actually add for customers, or is the value flowing only one direction?
How do you grandfather perpetual customers without building a two-class system forever?
What churn from the pivot is acceptable against the valuation upside — and whose spreadsheet decides?

title: The October Export Control Expansion
setup: October brings expanded export controls covering a chip category your products use, with a licensing regime for several markets that make up 18% of your revenue. Legal says some sales can continue under license; the timeline for licenses is 'indeterminate.'
questions: Do you keep serving the affected markets through the license process, or exit and stabilize?
What's the compliance exposure of getting an edge case wrong versus the revenue of pushing boundaries?
How does geopolitical exposure change your product architecture and market mix long-term?

title: November's Warranty Extension Play
setup: Going into holiday season, your biggest retail partner demands you fund an extended two-year warranty as a differentiator — their competitor's house brand offers one. Your failure-rate data says year two is where field failures concentrate.
questions: Do you know your true year-two failure cost, or has one-year warranty data hidden it from you?
Is the warranty a marketing cost or a product-quality bill coming due?
Could reliability investment cost less than warranty exposure within one product cycle?

title: The December Patent Troll Settlement Math
setup: A patent assertion entity sues you in December in a friendly jurisdiction, over a patent your counsel calls 'weak but not frivolous.' Settlement is $2M; litigation is $4M+ and two years of distraction, with a small chance of a nine-figure verdict.
questions: Does settling feed the machine that will sue you again, or is it just the rational price of doing business?
What joint-defense options exist with the other companies being sued on the same patent?
How does the small-chance-catastrophic-outcome risk actually get weighed, and by whom?

title: The January Return-to-Office Talent Test
setup: Your January engineering all-hands announces three days in office, aligning with peers. Within two weeks, four senior engineers — including the architect of your core platform — have offers from fully-remote competitors, and internal survey scores drop 20 points.
questions: Was the mandate solving a real collaboration problem or a visible-leadership problem?
What's the actual cost of losing the platform architect against whatever the office delivers?
Is there a policy that gets the collaboration you need without the exodus you're watching start?

title: February's Firmware Update Bricking Incident
setup: A February firmware update bricks an estimated 0.3% of devices in the field — thousands of units — due to a flash-memory batch variance your testing didn't cover. Affected customers are loud, and the fix requires physical service or replacement.
questions: Do you replace units no-questions-asked, and what does generosity cost versus what the story costs?
How did batch variance escape testing, and what does hardware-diversity coverage look like now?
Do you pause the update program entirely, knowing the update also patched a security flaw?

title: The Spring Benchmark Controversy
setup: In March, a respected reviewer discovers your device detects benchmark apps and boosts performance beyond sustainable levels — a legacy optimization from two product generations ago that nobody removed. The story frames it as cheating.
questions: Is the technical explanation — thermal headroom, legacy code — a defense or an excuse, and does the difference matter publicly?
Do you remove the optimization and accept lower benchmark scores against rivals who may do the same thing quietly?
What review of 'legacy optimizations' does this trigger before the next reviewer finds the next one?

title: A New EV Tax Credit Upends Pricing
setup: A new federal EV tax credit policy takes effect January 1st, changing which of your models qualify and by how much, overnight shifting the effective price customers see.
questions: How quickly can pricing and marketing materials reflect the new incentive structure?
Does this change which models you should be pushing dealers to prioritize?
What do you tell customers mid-negotiation when the incentive changed under them?

title: A Chip Shortage Halts a Production Line
setup: In February, a semiconductor shortage forces a temporary shutdown of one production line, threatening delivery timelines for a popular model already backordered.
questions: Do you idle the line, or shift to producing lower-tech trims that don't need the scarce chip?
How do you communicate delivery delays to customers who've already ordered?
Should chip sourcing diversification be a bigger priority after this?

title: A Recall Lands Before the Spring Sales Push
setup: In March, a safety issue is identified requiring a recall on a widely sold model, right as your spring sales campaign is set to launch.
questions: Do you delay the sales campaign, or run it separately from recall communications?
How do you rebuild trust with customers affected by the recall during a sales push?
What's the cost of the recall itself versus the reputational cost of poor handling?

title: A Competitor's Incentives Undercut Spring Sales
setup: In April, a rival automaker launches an unusually aggressive incentive program right in the middle of the spring sales season, and dealers are asking you to match it.
questions: Do you match the incentive, or hold pricing and compete on other value?
What's the margin cost of matching versus the volume cost of not matching?
How sustainable is this level of incentive if the competitor keeps it up all season?

title: A Key Supplier's Plant Floods
setup: In May, severe flooding shuts down a key parts supplier's plant, threatening your summer production schedule with no immediate alternative source identified.
questions: How much buffer inventory exists to absorb this disruption before production is affected?
How quickly can an alternate supplier be qualified for a critical part like this?
What does this reveal about single-source risk in your supply chain?

title: The Summer Retooling Runs Long
setup: Your planned June plant shutdown to retool for the new model year was scheduled for two weeks. It's now day twenty, delaying the launch timeline for the new model year vehicles.
questions: Do you push back the model year launch date, or compress the ramp-up timeline?
What does this delay cost in dealer floor plan financing and marketing timing?
What went wrong in the retooling estimate that should change next year's planning?

title: Rising Gas Prices Shift Demand Suddenly
setup: A sharp gas price spike in July shifts customer demand heavily toward your smaller and electric vehicle lineup, straining production capacity on lines that weren't sized for this demand level.
questions: Can production be reallocated quickly enough to meet the shifted demand?
How do you manage dealer inventory that's now mismatched with what customers actually want?
Should production planning build in more flexibility for demand shifts like this?

title: Union Talks Heat Up Before Fall Ramp
setup: Labor union contract negotiations intensify in August, right as fall production is scheduled to ramp up for the new model year, with a strike authorization vote looming.
questions: How do you plan production continuity if a strike disrupts the fall ramp?
What's the cost of a short-term concession versus a prolonged work stoppage during this critical window?
Should contract timing be renegotiated to avoid this seasonal collision going forward?

title: A Software Defect in the New Model Year
setup: As model year changeover begins in September, a software defect is discovered in the new infotainment system just as the first vehicles are rolling off the line.
questions: Do you halt shipments for a fix, or ship with a planned over-the-air update later?
How do you communicate this to dealers who are expecting vehicles imminently?
What does this reveal about software testing timelines relative to hardware production schedules?

title: A Rival Unveils Autonomous Features at the Auto Show
setup: At October's major auto show, a competitor unveils an autonomous-driving feature well ahead of your current roadmap, generating significant press attention and customer interest.
questions: Does this change your R&D investment priorities immediately?
How do you respond to customers and dealers asking why you don't have this yet?
Is this the right race to chase, or is your differentiation better placed elsewhere?

title: Holiday Demand Outpaces Dealer Inventory
setup: A Black Friday and holiday season sales event in November drives demand for your best-selling trim well beyond what dealers currently have in stock.
questions: How do you prioritize which dealers get limited inventory during a high-demand event?
What's the cost of lost sales during peak demand versus the cost of overproducing for future years?
Should demand forecasting for this trim have been more conservative or aggressive going into the season?

title: Rising Rates Squeeze Dealer Financing at Year-End
setup: In December, floor-plan financing rates rise just as dealers are pushing hardest for year-end sales volume, squeezing dealer margins right when they need the push the most.
questions: Do you offer manufacturer incentives to offset the higher financing cost for dealers?
How does this affect dealer appetite to carry inventory into the new year?
Should floor-plan support be built into year-end planning more proactively?

title: Winter Weather Delays the New Year Sales Push
setup: Severe winter weather in January disrupts vehicle logistics right after the new year sales campaign launched, delaying deliveries to dealers who were counting on fresh inventory.
questions: How do you manage dealer and customer expectations during a weather-driven delay?
Is winter logistics risk already built into how the new year sales campaign is planned each year?
What alternative transport or routing options exist when primary logistics are disrupted?

title: An Autonomous Driving Incident Triggers Investigation
setup: In March, an incident involving your vehicle's autonomous driving feature triggers a regulatory investigation, drawing significant media attention.
questions: What's your immediate communication strategy while facts are still being established?
How do you balance cooperation with regulators against protecting the company's position?
Does this change how aggressively you market this feature while the investigation is ongoing?

title: New Parts Tariffs Hit Fall Production
setup: In September, new tariffs on imported parts are announced right as fall production ramps up for the new model year, raising costs on vehicles already priced and marketed to dealers.
questions: Do you absorb the cost for this model year, or attempt a pricing adjustment mid-cycle?
How exposed is your parts sourcing to further tariff changes this year?
Should future model year pricing build in more buffer against policy risk like this?

title: January's Fleet Order Cliff
setup: Rental car companies and commercial fleets — 30% of your volume — signal in January that rising rates have them slashing this year's orders by 40%. Fleet sales carry thin margins but keep two plants at efficient utilization.
questions: Do you chase retail volume to fill the plants, cut shifts, or build inventory betting on recovery?
What does reduced plant utilization actually cost versus the margin on the fleet units lost?
Should fleet dependence at these two plants have been flagged as a structural risk earlier?

title: The February Dealer Franchise Lawsuit
setup: Your February announcement of direct online sales for one EV model triggers a lawsuit from your dealer council citing franchise law, and three state dealer associations join within a week. The EV customer data shows 70% prefer buying online.
questions: Is the direct model worth the franchise war, or is there a dealer-participating hybrid?
What do dealers actually add for an EV buyer, and what's honest to pay them for it?
How do the other 49 states' laws shape where this fight is even winnable?

title: March's Battery Plant Partner Wobble
setup: Your battery joint-venture partner misses a March capital call amid their own financial troubles, putting the plant that supplies your entire EV line at risk of construction delays — with your EV launch calendar built around its output.
questions: Do you fund their share, find a new partner, or slow the EV ramp to match reality?
What does the JV agreement actually let you do, and what does exercising those rights cost the relationship?
How exposed is your EV strategy to a partner you don't control?

title: The April Certified Pre-Owned Squeeze
setup: Three years of low new-car leasing during the supply crunch means April's off-lease return volume — the lifeblood of your certified pre-owned program — is down 50%. Dealers depend on CPO margins, and your used-supply pipeline won't recover for two years.
questions: Do you buy back customer vehicles aggressively to feed CPO, competing with your own auction proceeds?
What replaces CPO profit for dealers during the two-year gap?
How should today's leasing strategy change so this cycle doesn't repeat?

title: May's Connected Car Subscription Backlash
setup: Your May over-the-air update moves heated seats and remote start — hardware already in every vehicle — behind a monthly subscription for new buyers. The public reaction is savage, a consumer group files a complaint, and dealers report customers asking about it by name.
questions: Is feature-subscription revenue worth being the face of a practice customers viscerally hate?
What's the difference between subscription features customers accept and ones they see as ransom?
Do you reverse course publicly or ride it out, and what does each cost?

title: The June Heat Dome Battery Claims
setup: A June heat dome across the Southwest triggers a spike in EV battery degradation warranty claims from three model years, concentrated in vehicles that fast-charge frequently in extreme heat. Engineering says it's within design tolerance; customers watching their range shrink disagree.
questions: Does 'within design tolerance' survive contact with customers who lost 15% of range?
Do you adjust charging software to protect batteries at the cost of charging speed, and how do you message that?
What does the Southwest data mean for how you warranty batteries in a hotter future?

title: July's Plant Conversion Point of No Return
setup: July is the final decision gate for converting your midsize-SUV plant to EV production — an 18-month, $1.5B conversion planned when EV demand curves pointed straight up. Current EV demand is growing slower than the plan assumed, and the gas SUV it builds remains your most profitable vehicle.
questions: Do you convert on schedule, delay a year, or redesign for a flexible line that builds both?
What does each option cost in 2030 competitiveness versus this decade's cash flow?
Whose demand forecast do you trust now, after the last one missed?

title: The August Software Recall Precedent
setup: An August NHTSA inquiry asks why a driver-assist flaw you fixed via over-the-air update in June wasn't filed as a formal recall. Your position: it was a software improvement. Their position: any safety-related fix is a recall, with notification obligations, regardless of delivery method.
questions: Does calling OTA safety fixes 'recalls' change customer perception of updates — and should it?
What's the retroactive exposure for previous OTA fixes now, and do you self-report them?
How do software-era safety obligations get decided — by the letter of rules written for hardware, or negotiation?

title: September's Model Year Price Positioning
setup: New model year pricing locks in September, and the data conflicts: transaction prices are softening industry-wide, inventory days-on-lot are rising, but your input costs are up 4% and the new model year adds content customers demanded.
questions: Do you price for the market that's coming or the costs you're carrying?
Which added content actually commands price versus checked a survey box?
What's the incentive budget hiding in the pricing decision — and is a higher MSRP with bigger incentives honest?

title: The October Supplier Financial Distress Cascade
setup: October brings notice that a Tier 2 supplier — feeding three of your Tier 1s — is entering restructuring. None of your Tier 1 contracts require them to disclose their own suppliers' health, and your visibility ends one tier down.
questions: Do you bail out a supplier you have no contract with, to protect production you depend on?
What sub-tier visibility should contracts require after this, and will Tier 1s accept it?
Which of your vehicle lines has the most hidden sub-tier concentration risk right now — can you even answer that?

title: November's Insurance Data Partnership Exposure
setup: A November news investigation reveals that driving-behavior data from your connected vehicles, shared with a data broker under user agreements nobody reads, has been raising customers' insurance premiums. Attorneys general in two states open inquiries.
questions: Do you end the data-sharing program, and what contractual and revenue unwinding does that take?
What does informed consent for vehicle data honestly look like, versus what compliance allows?
How do you rebuild trust in connected services after customers learn their car testified against them?

title: The December Year-End Self-Registration Temptation
setup: December's sales race with your nearest competitor is within a few thousand units, and regional managers propose the industry's old trick: having dealers self-register inventory as sold to book the units this year. It's legal, it's done, and it borrows January's sales to win December's headline.
questions: What does winning the annual sales crown actually buy, and who inside the company is it really for?
What does pushed metal do to January's real demand, dealer margins, and residual values?
If the practice is fine, why does everyone involved prefer it not be explained publicly?

title: The January Charging Standard Switch
setup: In January, the industry's momentum toward your competitor's charging connector becomes undeniable — two more automakers switch, and major charging networks announce they'll prioritize it. Your vehicles use the other standard, and your customers' adapter experience is poor.
questions: Do you switch connectors mid-product-cycle, and what does the transition do to existing owners?
What do you owe the customers who bought vehicles on the losing standard?
How did the standards bet get made, and what does the miss teach about platform decisions ahead — like charging curves and battery formats?

title: February's Crash Test Asterisk
setup: A February safety-ratings refresh gives your family SUV top marks — except a new test protocol, being phased in, where it scores marginal. Marketing wants to advertise the top rating; the marginal score applies to a rear-seat scenario involving children.
questions: Do you advertise the headline rating while a child-safety scenario scores marginal?
What's the engineering fix timeline, and does it wait for the next model year or interrupt this one?
When the new protocol becomes standard and the score becomes public, what will this year's advertising look like in hindsight?

title: The March Tariff Retaliation Squeeze
setup: March trade escalation puts retaliatory tariffs on vehicles you export to a major market — while your competitor builds locally there and is untouched. Your export volume to that market supports an entire shift at your flagship plant.
questions: Do you eat the tariff to hold market share, raise prices and cede ground, or commit to local production?
How long do you protect the flagship plant's shift while the trade situation stays unresolved?
What's the trigger point — time or money — where this stops being a storm to weather and becomes a structure to exit?

title: Post-Holiday Returns Eat January's Margin
setup: It's the second week of January and returns are running 22% of December sales — five points above plan. Restocking labor is maxed out, and a third of returned goods can't go back on shelves at full price.
questions: Do you liquidate returned stock fast at a loss, or hold it and tie up working capital?
Which December promotions drove the worst return behavior, and would you run them again?
Should return policy tighten next year, and what would that cost in holiday conversion?

title: The Valentine's Buy Was Wrong
setup: Ten days before Valentine's Day, sell-through on your gift assortment is half the forecast, but one unadvertised SKU is nearly sold out. There's one reorder window left with your supplier — decisions due in 48 hours.
questions: Do you chase the surprise winner with a rush reorder, or protect margin and let it sell out?
How do you clear the slow gift stock before it becomes worthless on February 15th?
What signal did the buying team miss, and how do you catch it next year?

title: Spring Reset vs. a Late Winter
setup: Your March floor reset to spring assortment is scheduled, but a cold snap has winter goods still selling at full price. Corporate wants the reset on schedule; store managers want two more weeks.
questions: Who should own this call — merchandising calendar or store-level sell-through data?
What does an early reset cost in markdowns versus a late reset in spring launch momentum?
Could reset timing flex by region instead of being one national date?

title: A Tariff Lands Mid-Season
setup: In April, a new tariff adds 18% to landed cost on goods already on the water for your summer set. Prices are printed, promotions are booked, and your two largest competitors haven't moved yet.
questions: Do you reprice mid-season, eat the margin, or split the difference on select SKUs?
Does being first or last to raise prices matter more in your category?
What sourcing shifts would insulate next year's buy, and when must those decisions be made?

title: Mother's Day Weekend Staffing Collapse
setup: The Friday before Mother's Day — your second-biggest gifting weekend — a flu wave takes out 30% of scheduled floor staff. Overtime is available but pushes the week's labor 15% over budget.
questions: Do you protect the customer experience with overtime, or hold the labor line during peak revenue?
Which service points get cut first when you can't cover everything — fitting rooms, registers, floor help?
What cross-training would have made this a non-event?

title: The Anchor Store Next Door Closes
setup: In June, the anchor tenant that drives half your mall location's foot traffic announces it's closing in 60 days. Your lease runs three more years with no co-tenancy clause.
questions: Do you negotiate rent relief now, plan an exit, or invest to become a destination yourself?
How fast will traffic actually decay, and what data would tell you early?
What does this teach you about lease terms for future locations?

title: Prime Day Pulls Your July Forward
setup: A major online retailer's July sales event is training your customers to expect deep mid-summer discounts. Last year you matched it and gave back six points of margin; sitting it out cost a competitor 9% of the month's traffic.
questions: Do you compete in someone else's promotional moment or build your own?
Which categories can win on convenience and service rather than price that week?
What did last year's match actually buy you — new customers or discounted regulars?

title: Back-to-School Inventory Is Stuck in Port
setup: It's late July and 40% of your back-to-school assortment is delayed at port, with best-case arrival two weeks into the season. Air freight would recover ten days but wipe out the category's margin.
questions: Do you air-freight the hero SKUs, all of it, or none of it?
How do you keep back-to-school shoppers who find empty shelves from defecting for the whole season?
Should the BTS buy ship earlier next year even at higher warehousing cost?

title: The Labor Day Weekend Systems Outage
setup: Your POS system vendor pushes a faulty update Friday night of Labor Day weekend. Stores open Saturday to registers that take 90 seconds per transaction, and lines are out the door by 11 a.m.
questions: What's the in-store playbook right now — line-busting, manual fallback, free coffee?
How hard do you push the vendor on liability, and does this end the relationship?
What's your tolerance for single-vendor dependence in systems that can stop revenue cold?

title: Holiday Hiring in a Tight Labor Market
setup: It's September and applications for seasonal roles are down 40% year over year. A competing chain is advertising $3/hour more plus a completion bonus. You need 200 seasonal hires by November 1st.
questions: Do you match on wage, differentiate on schedule flexibility, or lower the hiring bar?
What does an understaffed December actually cost compared to the wage premium?
Could year-round part-timers with guaranteed holiday hours replace some of the seasonal scramble?

title: The October Forecast Split
setup: Your holiday forecast meeting produces a 12% gap between planning's cautious number and sales' aggressive one. The final buy commits this week. Overbuying means January markdowns; underbuying means stockouts in the two weeks that make your year.
questions: Which error is cheaper in your business — too much inventory or too little?
Can the assortment be structured so part of the buy is reorderable in-season?
Whose forecast has been closer historically, and why doesn't that settle it?

title: Black Friday Doorbuster Backfires
setup: Your headline Black Friday doorbuster sells out in 40 minutes. Social media fills with angry posts from customers who waited hours, and local news picks it up by noon — with Cyber Monday still ahead.
questions: What do you offer the customers who walked away empty-handed, and how publicly?
Was the doorbuster quantity a planning failure or a deliberate scarcity play that worked too well?
How does this change what you promote for Cyber Monday 72 hours from now?

title: December 23rd Delivery Promise at Risk
setup: Your carrier notifies you on December 19th that a storm system will break the delivery promise on roughly 8,000 online orders guaranteed by Christmas Eve.
questions: Do you notify customers proactively today or wait for carrier confirmation?
What compensation is right for a broken Christmas promise — refund shipping, gift cards, or more?
Should the guaranteed-by-Christmas cutoff have been earlier, even at the cost of a slower final week?

title: Gift Card Liability Comes Due
setup: January redemption data shows gift card usage running 30% above last year, and redeemers are skewing toward clearance items — meaning December's 'sales' are converting into January's low-margin transactions.
questions: How should gift card economics change how you value December's topline?
Can January's redemption traffic be steered toward full-price categories?
Does the gift card push deserve the same marketing investment next year?

title: The Loyalty Data Breach
setup: In February, your loyalty program vendor discloses a breach exposing emails and purchase histories of 400,000 members. Legal says notification is required within 30 days; marketing had a spring campaign built entirely on that program.
questions: How do you notify members in a way that preserves the program's future?
Does the spring campaign proceed, pause, or get rebuilt around a trust message?
What vendor security obligations should have been contractual, and what do you change now?

title: Shrink Spikes in Three Stores
setup: March inventory counts show shrink at triple the chain average in three urban stores. The options on the table — locked cases, guards, reduced hours — each trade theft against sales and staff morale differently.
questions: Which intervention do you pilot first, and what would success look like in 90 days?
At what shrink level does a store stop making economic sense?
How do you address this without making honest customers feel policed?

title: Your Private Label Outsells the National Brand
setup: By April, your private-label line outsells the national brand it imitates in 60% of stores — and that brand's rep just hinted they may pull distribution, which would cost you their traffic-driving ad spend.
questions: Do you protect the national-brand relationship or press the private-label advantage?
What happens to the category if they actually walk?
How much of private label's success depends on the brand it shadows sitting next to it?

title: The Influencer Spike You Didn't Plan
setup: In May, a video featuring one of your mid-tier products goes viral. The SKU sells out chain-wide in four days, resellers are flipping it at 3x, and your supplier needs eight weeks for a meaningful reorder.
questions: Do you place a big reorder betting the demand lasts eight weeks, or ride the moment and move on?
How do you convert this accidental audience into customers for the rest of the assortment?
Is there a fast-follow product or bundle you could ship while attention is high?

title: Father's Day Falls Flat
setup: Father's Day week in June comes in 15% under plan chain-wide, continuing a three-year slide in the holiday's performance, while the same week's non-gift categories grew.
questions: Is this a merchandising failure or a genuinely fading occasion?
Should next June's plan shrink the gift set and reallocate the space and spend?
What occasions are growing that deserve the investment this one is losing?

title: The Sidewalk Construction Summer
setup: The city announces in July that the street fronting your flagship will be torn up through October — peak tourist season. The last comparable project cut affected retailers' traffic by a third.
questions: What's the mitigation plan — signage, rear entrance, delivery push, pop-up elsewhere?
Do you pursue the city's business-interruption relief, and is the paperwork worth it?
Which flagship functions (revenue, brand, events) can be temporarily moved online or off-site?

title: The Self-Checkout Decision
setup: In August, the board asks for a self-checkout rollout plan projected to cut front-end labor 25%. Your two pilot stores show faster throughput, a measurable shrink increase, and mixed customer feedback split sharply by age.
questions: Does the labor saving survive the shrink increase in your actual numbers?
Is checkout a cost center to automate or a service moment that differentiates you?
If you proceed, what happens to the displaced hours — cut, or redeployed to the floor?

title: Halloween Category Went Early
setup: It's mid-September and your Halloween set has been on the floor two weeks — but a competitor set theirs in mid-August and captured the early-bird spend. Your sell-through is tracking 20% behind last year with the peak weeks still ahead.
questions: Is the early-set arms race worth joining next year, and what does it displace?
Can the remaining six weeks recover the gap, or should markdowns start now?
What does 'seasonal creep' do to the categories that lose floor time to it?

title: The November Price-Match Trap
setup: Your price-match policy, written for a calmer era, is being systematically exploited during November's promo season — customers matching flash prices from online sellers with no local presence. Store managers are eating the difference or arguing at the register.
questions: Do you honor the policy's letter through the holidays and fix it in January, or change it mid-season?
What does a register-level pricing argument cost you in December versus the match itself?
What should the policy's next version actually promise?

title: Christmas Eve Skeleton Crew
setup: Christmas Eve is a top-five revenue day for last-minute shoppers, but two-thirds of staff requested it off and corporate guidance says close at 6 p.m. Your busiest competitor stays open until 10.
questions: What's the revenue of hours 6-to-10 worth against what it costs the people working them?
Could a volunteer premium-pay crew cover the late shift without mandating anyone?
Does matching the competitor's hours matter, or is that their customer, not yours?

title: The January Store Closure List
setup: Year-end P&Ls put four of your 30 stores below the closure threshold. But one anchors your presence in a growing metro, one just got a new manager showing momentum, and closures announced in January hit the press as a 'struggling chain' story.
questions: Which of the four actually close, and what earns an exception?
How do you sequence announcements to control the narrative?
What early-warning metrics would have flagged these stores two years sooner?

title: Buy-Online-Pickup-In-Store Is Breaking
setup: By February, BOPIS has grown to 20% of online orders, but pickup wait times average nine minutes, staff pull inventory from the same shelves customers shop, and stockout-after-purchase rates are climbing.
questions: Does BOPIS get dedicated staffing and back-room inventory, and what does that cost?
At what wait time does the channel start destroying more loyalty than it builds?
Is BOPIS a convenience feature or a fundamentally different operating model you've been running as a side task?

title: The Easter Timing Whiplash
setup: Easter lands three weeks earlier than last year, compressing your spring season. Last year's late-Easter comps make this March look great and April look terrible, and the board reads month-over-month numbers literally.
questions: How do you plan and report around a floating holiday so comps stay honest?
Which buys and promotions actually need to move with Easter, and which just follow the calendar?
What other floating events distort your comps, and does everyone reading the numbers know it?

title: A Union Drive at the Distribution Center
setup: In May, organizers file for a union election at the distribution center that feeds all 30 stores, with a vote set for late June — five weeks before your back-to-school inventory surge.
questions: What can leadership legally say and do, and who needs that briefing today?
What grievances are driving this, and which are fixable regardless of the vote's outcome?
What's the contingency if a work action hits during the BTS ramp?

title: The Resale Platform Question
setup: In July, your brand's products are trading briskly on resale platforms — sometimes above retail for discontinued items. A platform approaches you about an official certified-resale partnership taking a revenue share.
questions: Does official resale extend your brand or cannibalize new-product sales?
Who is buying resale — your customer trading down, or a new customer entering the brand?
What does the discontinued-items premium tell you about your product and reorder decisions?

title: Weather Says Warm, the Buy Says Coats
setup: Long-range forecasts in October predict the warmest winter in a decade. Your cold-weather buy — committed in June — is your highest-margin category, and the first markdowns of last year's warm winter started a chain-wide margin slide.
questions: Do you act on a seasonal forecast now — early promotions, inventory transfers south — or wait for actual weather?
Which stores or regions can absorb reallocated cold-weather stock?
Should weather risk change how the winter buy is structured — later commitments, more reorder flexibility, less depth?
